proposals.title: Learning from Heterogeneous Demonstrations: A Policy Architecture for Robots with Incommensurate Action Spaces
proposals.motivation: Current imitation learning and human-robot interaction methods often assume a unified, shared action space representation across different robot embodiments. This assumption breaks down in real-world settings where robots with vastly different morphologies (e.g., a 7-DoF arm vs. a mobile manipulator) must learn from the same pool of human demonstrations. Pre-2026, methods like Behavioral Cloning or DAPG struggle without this mapping, limiting scalable, cross-platform learning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a policy can be learned directly from heterogeneous demonstrations by first inferring the latent human intent behind each demonstration, and then grounding that intent in the specific action space of the target robot, thereby bypassing the need for a unified action representation.
proposals.method: We propose a two-stage architecture. First, a VLM-based (e.g., CLIP or Flamingo) or graph neural network encoder will map raw demonstration trajectories (vision, proprioception, language descriptions) into a shared, robot-agnostic intent embedding space. Second, a robot-specific decoder, conditioned on this intent embedding and the robot's own state, will output low-level actions using techniques like diffusion policies or transformer-based sequence models. Training will leverage multi-task learning across robots and a contrastive loss to align intents from different demonstration modalities.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel framework for cross-embodiment imitation learning that does not require action space alignment. It will provide a benchmark for heterogeneous demonstration learning and advance the field toward more flexible and scalable robot teaching from diverse human teachers and existing datasets.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work on multi-robot learning or modality-aligned demonstrations, our method explicitly avoids constructing a shared action space, instead learning to translate through a shared semantic intent space, which is a more natural and flexible abstraction for human instruction.

proposals.title: Zero-Shot Robot Skill Adaptation via Compositional Language-Embedded Skill Priors
proposals.motivation: Deploying robots in diverse real-world settings requires adapting to new hardware and tasks with minimal data, but current imitation and reinforcement learning methods are data-hungry. Pre-2026, large vision-language models (VLMs) show remarkable zero-shot reasoning, yet their knowledge is not easily grounded in physical robot embodiment and control. This work addresses the critical gap of leveraging these models for effective, data-efficient robot skill transfer.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that decomposing tasks into a library of language-anchored, embodiment-abstract skill primitives, learned from diverse multi-robot demonstrations, will enable a robot to recompose these primitives for zero-shot execution on novel tasks and unseen robot morphologies.
proposals.method: We will train a compositional skill prior model on a large, heterogeneous dataset of robot demonstrations (e.g., RoboNet, Open X-Embodiment) paired with natural language descriptions. The model architecture will be a transformer-based VLM that maps images and language instructions to a discrete skill codebook and associated parameterized policy sketches. For a new robot, we will use a low-dimensional embodiment descriptor (e.g., joint/action space dimensionality, end-effector type) to condition a lightweight policy network that translates the abstract skill codes and parameters into executable actions, requiring only a few demonstrations or none for calibration.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel framework for zero-shot cross-embodiment skill transfer, reducing the need for task-specific fine-tuning. It will provide a benchmark for evaluating compositional generalization in robot learning and demonstrate how to effectively ground the semantic knowledge of VLMs into actionable, robot-specific policies.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work that uses VLMs for high-level planning or single-robot imitation, our approach novelly learns a disentangled, language-grounded skill space explicitly designed for compositional generalization across both tasks and robot embodiments, moving beyond task-level to skill-level transfer.

proposals.title: Decentralized Continual Imitation Learning from Heterogeneous Human-Robot Interactions
proposals.motivation: Deploying robots in diverse, real-world settings (e.g., homes, hospitals) requires policies that can adapt to new tasks and user preferences over time. Current imitation learning paradigms rely on centralized, curated datasets or single-robot demonstrations, which do not scale and fail to capture the long-tail of heterogeneous interactions 'in the wild'. A method for continual, decentralized learning is needed to enable robust and personalized robot assistance.
proposals.hypothesis: A decentralized learning framework that aggregates heterogeneous, on-policy demonstrations from a fleet of robots via a shared, parameter-efficient model will enable continual adaptation and outperform static, centrally-trained policies on long-tail tasks.
proposals.method: We propose a method where each robot runs an on-policy imitation learning algorithm (e.g., DAgger) locally, collecting corrective demonstrations from its specific user. A central server will host a base policy, likely a vision-language model (VLM) conditioned on language instructions, similar to scaffolding approaches. Each robot will maintain a lightweight adapter module (e.g., LoRA) fine-tuned on its local data. These adapters will be periodically aggregated and distilled into the central model using techniques from federated learning, ensuring continual learning without catastrophic forgetting.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel federated imitation learning framework for robotics, 2) empirical validation on a simulated heterogeneous robot fleet performing language-conditioned tasks, and 3) analysis of trade-offs between personalization, generalization, and communication costs in continual robot learning.
proposals.novelty: While federated learning exists, its application to continual, on-policy imitation learning from real human-robot interaction—integrating VLMs for instruction following and adapter-based personalization—is unexplored and addresses key scalability challenges for real-world deployment.

proposals.title: Autonomous Task Discovery and Skill Acquisition via Intrinsic Motivation and Self-Supervised Learning
proposals.motivation: Current robot learning paradigms rely heavily on explicit human specification, such as language commands or goal images, which limits autonomy and scalability. In unstructured environments like homes, a robot must be able to identify and learn useful tasks without constant instruction. This research addresses the fundamental challenge of enabling robots to autonomously discover and pursue meaningful objectives, a key step toward general-purpose assistive agents.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by combining unsupervised skill discovery with a learned model of human preferences, a robot can autonomously generate and execute a curriculum of tasks that are both learnable and useful, without any explicit language or goal specification.
proposals.method: We propose a two-phase approach. First, we use unsupervised reinforcement learning with an information-theoretic intrinsic motivation objective (e.g., empowerment or diversity-based exploration) to learn a diverse repertoire of low-level skills in a simulated environment. Second, we train a preference model on passive, unlabeled human video demonstrations (e.g., from Ego4D or Epic-Kitchens datasets) to predict which discovered skills are 'useful' or 'human-like'. This model will guide the robot's autonomous task selection and skill refinement.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel framework for autonomous task discovery that decouples skill learning from explicit human specification, 2) a method for grounding 'usefulness' in passive human observation rather than active feedback, and 3) empirical benchmarks for evaluating autonomous task discovery in simulated household environments.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior imitation or instruction-following work, our agent receives no demonstrations for its own actions and no explicit goals. The novelty lies in the closed-loop coupling of unsupervised skill discovery with a passively learned human preference model to autonomously generate a task curriculum.

proposals.title: Scaling Laws for Embodied Reasoning: The Effect of Model and Data Scale on Robot Policy Generalization
proposals.motivation: Recent work, such as RT-2 and our own on VLM scaffolding, shows that large-scale pre-trained vision-language models (VLMs) can enable robots to perform a variety of tasks. However, the scaling laws governing embodied AI—how performance on complex, long-horizon robot tasks improves with model size and the diversity of robot demonstration data—remain poorly understood. This is critical for efficiently allocating resources to build generalist robot policies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that scaling both the transformer model size and the diversity of robot demonstration data will yield super-linear improvements in the generalization and sample efficiency of learned policies for long-horizon, language-instructed manipulation tasks, following predictable scaling laws.
proposals.method: We will conduct a controlled scaling study using a transformer-based architecture (e.g., a modified RT-2) for policy learning. We will systematically vary model parameters (from hundreds of millions to tens of billions) and the scale/diversity of training data (e.g., combining datasets like Bridge, Language-Table, and our own teleoperation demonstrations). We will train policies via imitation learning (behavior cloning) and fine-tune with offline reinforcement learning (e.g., Conservative Q-Learning) on a suite of simulated and real-world manipulation tasks, measuring performance, generalization to novel instructions, and data efficiency.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first empirical scaling laws for embodied transformer policies, providing a quantitative guide for future research and deployment. It will also release a large-scale, multi-modal robot dataset and a family of pre-trained models that serve as a strong baseline for generalist robot learning.
proposals.novelty: While scaling laws are established for language and vision models, their systematic study for embodied, multi-modal action-generation models is novel. Furthermore, we uniquely investigate the interplay between architectural scale and the *diversity* of robot data, not just its volume.

proposals.title: Compositional Skill Planning with Language-Guided Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning
proposals.motivation: Enabling robots to perform complex, long-horizon tasks in unstructured environments remains a core challenge in robotics. While imitation learning and language models can provide individual skills or high-level instructions, they often fail at robustly sequencing and grounding these skills over extended time horizons. This work addresses the critical gap between high-level language understanding and low-level, long-term policy execution for task composition.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a hierarchical policy, where a high-level language-conditioned planner decomposes tasks into skill sequences and a low-level library of learned skills executes them, will significantly improve success rates on long-horizon tasks compared to flat end-to-end policies, especially when trained with multi-modal human demonstrations.
proposals.method: We propose a two-tier architecture. The high-level planner will be a transformer-based model, trained via offline reinforcement learning (e.g., IQL) on language-annotated demonstration trajectories, to output sequences of discrete skill identifiers and arguments. The low-level will consist of a library of skill-specific policies, trained from segmented demonstrations using methods like Diffusion Policy or BC-RNN. We will integrate a Vision-Language Model (VLM) for visual grounding of skill arguments and scaffold the training using the planner's output to guide low-level skill chaining and correction.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel hierarchical framework for language-guided long-horizon task decomposition, 2) a systematic evaluation benchmark for compositional task planning in simulation and on a physical robot arm, and 3) insights into how demonstration modality (e.g., video vs. teleoperation) affects the learnability of composable skill libraries.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the tight integration of language-conditioned hierarchical RL for planning with a skill library bootstrapped from multi-modal demonstrations, moving beyond using VLMs only for high-level instruction or using RL only for flat policies. This explicitly addresses the compositionality and grounding problem in long-horizon execution.

proposals.title: Specializing Generalist Policies: Achieving Super-Human Dexterity on a Single Robot Platform via Multi-Modal Imitation and Language-Guided Refinement
proposals.motivation: Recent advances in robotics have focused on developing generalist models capable of performing many tasks across diverse embodiments. However, this breadth often comes at the cost of peak performance on any single platform, leaving a critical gap in achieving reliable, super-human proficiency on complex, real-world manipulation tasks. Focusing on a single, capable platform (e.g., a dexterous hand-arm system) allows us to push the limits of precision and robustness, which is essential for applications like advanced manufacturing and assistive robotics.
proposals.hypothesis: By specializing a generalist vision-language-action policy, initially trained on diverse demonstrations, onto a single robot platform through targeted, high-quality imitation learning and language-conditioned reinforcement learning, we can surpass human-level performance on a suite of benchmark dexterous manipulation tasks.
proposals.method: We will adapt a pre-trained VLA policy (e.g., RT-2) to a specific platform like the Allegro Hand on a Franka arm. First, we will collect a dense, multi-modal dataset of expert demonstrations (kinesthetic, VR) for target tasks. We will then fine-tune the policy using a hybrid objective combining behavior cloning on this data and a language-conditioned, multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) objective, where multiple policy instances compete/cooperate in simulation to discover refinements. The language conditioning will allow for explicit human feedback and task specification during online refinement.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver: 1) a methodology for specializing generalist models to achieve super-human performance on a specific robot, 2) a new benchmark of super-human performance scores on complex dexterous manipulation tasks, and 3) insights into the trade-offs between generality and specialization in robot learning. It will demonstrate a clear path from broad-capability models to high-reliability deployment.
proposals.novelty: While prior work scaffolds manipulation with VLMs or studies demonstration modality, the explicit focus on specializing a generalist policy to exceed human demonstration quality on one platform using a MARL-based refinement process is novel. It inverts the typical scaling narrative by focusing on depth over breadth to achieve a new performance regime.

proposals.title: Learning to Act from Multimodal Demonstrations: A Unified Framework for Video, Image, and Language-Guided Imitation
proposals.motivation: Current imitation learning methods often rely on a single demonstration modality, such as kinesthetic teaching or action-labeled videos, which limits their ability to learn from the rich, multimodal data humans naturally produce. To build robots that can learn from diverse human input—like instructional videos, annotated images, or descriptive text—we need agents that can reason across these modalities to infer and execute tasks. This capability is crucial for scaling robot learning to unstructured environments where explicit, single-modality demonstrations are impractical.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a unified representation, jointly trained on video demonstrations, image-based task specifications, and natural language instructions, will enable more data-efficient and generalizable imitation learning than models trained on any single modality. This cross-modal grounding will allow an agent to infer a task's intent and dynamics from complementary information sources.
proposals.method: We will develop a transformer-based architecture with modality-specific encoders (e.g., a video encoder, a vision-language model for images+text, and a text encoder) projecting into a shared latent space. This shared representation will be trained via a multi-task objective combining: 1) contrastive learning to align modalities for the same task, 2) behavior cloning loss on action sequences from video demonstrations, and 3) a goal-conditioned reinforcement learning objective where the goal is derived from the multimodal context. We will evaluate on a suite of simulated and real-robot manipulation tasks with paired multimodal demonstrations.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, unified framework for multimodal imitation learning that explicitly models the synergy between video, image, and language. It will provide empirical evidence on how different demonstration modalities complement each other for task learning and generalization. The resulting models will enable robots to learn from a broader, more accessible range of human-provided data.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has explored combining language with demonstrations or using vision-language models for planning, our approach uniquely focuses on learning a single policy that can be conditioned on *any combination* of video, image, and language inputs, treating them as interchangeable or composable task specifications for imitation. This moves beyond using language models merely for high-level planning to deeply fusing multimodal understanding for low-level control.

proposals.title: Robust Preference Learning via Multi-Modal Adversarial Regularization for Human-Robot Interaction
proposals.motivation: Reward models trained via preference learning are central to aligning robot behavior with human intent, but they often fail catastrophically on out-of-distribution (OOD) states or are easily fooled by adversarial inputs. This fragility limits the safe deployment of learned policies in real-world human-robot interaction, where novel situations and ambiguous human feedback are common. Improving the generalization and robustness of these reward models is therefore a critical bottleneck for reliable robot learning from human input.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a reward model trained with a multi-modal, adversarially regularized objective will exhibit significantly improved generalization to OOD states and robustness to adversarial perturbations in human-provided feedback compared to standard training from demonstrations.
proposals.method: We will develop a training framework that integrates language-conditioned reward models (inspired by VLM scaffolding) with adversarial regularization. Specifically, we will train a reward model on a mixture of human demonstrations and preference labels, while simultaneously training a generative adversarial network (GAN) to produce challenging OOD state-action pairs and deceptive language instructions. The reward model will be regularized to output smooth, uncertainty-calibrated scores for these adversarial examples, enforcing consistency across modalities. We will evaluate generalization using held-out task families in simulation and physical robot manipulation, and robustness via targeted attacks on the feedback channel.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel, practical method for training robust, generalizable reward models for robot learning, 2) a benchmark for evaluating reward model robustness in human-robot interaction scenarios, and 3) empirical evidence linking multi-modal training and adversarial exposure to improved alignment safety.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has explored robustness in imitation learning or single-modality reward learning, the novel integration of multi-modal (vision-language) adversarial regularization specifically for preference-based reward models is unexplored. This directly addresses the unique vulnerability of learning from human feedback, which is central to Sadigh's research themes.

proposals.title: Inverse Reward Learning from RLHF: Recovering Latent Human Preferences from Aligned Language Models
proposals.motivation: RLHF has become the dominant method for aligning large language models (LLMs) with complex human values, but the learned reward model remains a black-box proxy. This opacity hinders interpretability, safety auditing, and the transfer of learned values to new domains or robotic systems. Extracting the latent preference structure from an RLHF-trained model is crucial for understanding what values the model has internalized and for creating more transparent and verifiable alignment pipelines.
proposals.hypothesis: The policy and value functions of an RLHF-aligned model encode a recoverable representation of the human preference model that guided its training. By inverting the RLHF process, we can infer this latent reward function with greater fidelity than the original proxy reward model.
proposals.method: We propose an inverse reinforcement learning (IRL) framework, treating the RLHF-fine-tuned LLM as an optimal policy. We will adapt adversarial IRL methods (e.g., AIRL) and trajectory-matching approaches to operate in the language domain, using the model's generations as 'expert demonstrations'. The core technical challenge is scaling IRL to high-dimensional sequence spaces; we will address this by leveraging the model's internal representations (e.g., final transformer layer outputs or value function estimates) as a structured state space for reward inference, and by using contrastive learning to distinguish preferred from dispreferred outputs.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel method for auditing and interpreting RLHF-aligned models, moving alignment from a black-box process towards a more transparent one. It will yield a formal framework for 'value distillation' from LLMs, enabling the extraction of portable preference models that could be used to guide the training of other agents, such as robots, with explicitly defined human values.
proposals.novelty: While IRL is well-established in robotics, applying it to recover preferences from RLHF-trained LLMs is novel. This inverts the standard RLHF pipeline, aiming to extract a more generalizable and interpretable reward function than the one used during training, moving beyond simple behavioral cloning of model outputs.

proposals.title: Learning to Query: Training Task-Specific Controllers with a Frozen Vision-Language Model as a World Model
proposals.motivation: Current methods for adapting Vision-Language Action Models (VLAs) to new robotic tasks typically involve fine-tuning the entire model, which is computationally expensive, risks catastrophic forgetting of general knowledge, and requires large task-specific datasets. A more efficient and scalable paradigm is needed to leverage the rich world knowledge and reasoning capabilities of large, pre-trained VLAs for precise, real-time control without modifying their core parameters.
proposals.hypothesis: A small, trainable controller can learn to generate effective queries to a frozen, generalist VLA, treating it as a predictive world model to guide actions, achieving superior sample efficiency and task performance compared to full fine-tuning, especially in low-data regimes.
proposals.method: We will train a lightweight neural network controller (e.g., a small MLP or transformer) that takes current visual and proprioceptive state as input and outputs both an action and a structured query (e.g., a text prompt or latent code) for a frozen VLA (like RT-2). The VLA, conditioned on the query and visual context, will predict the next state or the value of the proposed action. This prediction will be used as an intrinsic reward or a planning constraint within a reinforcement learning (e.g., soft actor-critic) or imitation learning objective to train the controller. The controller learns to ask informative questions (e.g., "what happens if I push this?" or "is this grasp stable?") that guide the VLA to provide supervision for the action policy.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a novel, parameter-efficient framework for robotic skill acquisition that decouples general world knowledge from task-specific control. It will provide empirical evidence on the sample efficiency and performance of this "inverted" paradigm across diverse manipulation benchmarks. The resulting methodology will enable faster robot adaptation and safer deployment by preserving the integrity and safety features of the base VLA.
proposals.novelty: Instead of fine-tuning the VLA for the task, we invert the relationship: we train a controller to *use* the frozen VLA as a tool. This shifts the learning problem from modifying world knowledge to learning optimal interrogation strategies, a perspective not explored in prior VLA robotics work.

proposals.title: Efficient Adaptation of Vision-Language-Action Models: Benchmarking and Designing Task-Specific PEFT for Robotic Control
proposals.motivation: Large Vision-Language-Action Models (VLAs) show promise for generalizable robot control but are prohibitively expensive to fully fine-tune for new tasks or environments. While Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) methods like LoRA and prefix-tuning exist for pure language or vision models, their efficacy and optimal design for the unique multi-modal, action-generating architecture of VLAs remain unexplored. Efficient adaptation is critical for deploying these models in diverse real-world robotic settings where data and compute are limited.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that novel PEFT architectures, which selectively adapt the cross-modal attention layers and action prediction heads of a frozen VLA, will achieve task performance comparable to full fine-tuning while using <5% of trainable parameters and significantly less demonstration data.
proposals.method: We will establish a benchmark using a pre-trained VLA (e.g., RT-2) and diverse robotic manipulation tasks. We will systematically evaluate standard PEFT methods (LoRA, Adapters, Prompt Tuning) on this benchmark. Building on these insights, we will design a novel 'Cross-Modal Action Adapter' that injects lightweight trainable modules into the visual-language fusion blocks and the final action decoder. This adapter will be trained using a combination of human demonstrations and language instructions, optimizing for both task success and sample efficiency.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic benchmark of PEFT methods for VLAs in robotics, offering clear guidelines for efficient adaptation. It will also contribute a novel adapter architecture tailored for the VLA paradigm, enabling rapid customization of foundation models for new robotic tasks with minimal data and compute.
proposals.novelty: While PEFT is established in NLP and vision, its application to the emergent VLA architecture for action generation is novel. Our work moves beyond simply applying existing methods by designing a new adapter informed by the specific information flow (vision->language->action) within VLAs.

proposals.title: Diffusion Policies for Vision-Language-Action Models: Improving Multi-Modal Action Generation in Human-Robot Interaction
proposals.motivation: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models, which map visual observations and language instructions to robot actions, are typically limited by their autoregressive action decoders. These decoders struggle to capture the inherent multi-modality of action sequences—where multiple valid action trajectories can achieve the same goal—leading to suboptimal and unstable policies. As pre-2026 research in Sadigh's lab has shown, the modality of human demonstration critically impacts imitation learning performance, suggesting a need for more expressive action generation models that can better leverage diverse human data.
proposals.hypothesis: Replacing the standard autoregressive action decoder in a VLA model with a conditional diffusion process will generate more diverse and higher-quality action sequences by explicitly modeling the multi-modal distribution over actions, thereby improving task success and robustness in interactive robot learning tasks.
proposals.method: We will adapt a pre-trained VLA model (e.g., based on RT-2 architecture) by replacing its final transformer-based action decoder with a diffusion policy network. The model will be conditioned on visual embeddings (from a ViT) and language embeddings (from an LLM). We will train the diffusion model using a denoising objective on human demonstration datasets (e.g., Bridge, Language-Table), where noise is iteratively removed from action sequences over a diffusion timestep. Inference will use classifier-free guidance to sample diverse action trajectories from the learned distribution.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, non-autoregressive action generation paradigm for VLAs, demonstrating quantifiable improvements in task success rates and behavioral diversity on benchmark manipulation tasks. It will contribute a methodological framework for integrating diffusion models into embodied AI, specifically addressing the challenge of learning from multi-modal human demonstrations.
proposals.novelty: While diffusion models have been explored for low-level control and trajectory generation, their integration as the core action decoder within a unified VLA architecture for language-conditioned, long-horizon tasks is novel. This directly addresses the expressivity limitations of standard autoregressive decoders in existing robot learning models.

proposals.title: Scaling Robot Learning to Long-Horizon, Multi-Objective Tasks via Compositional Imitation and Language-Guided Refinement
proposals.motivation: Current imitation learning and vision-language model (VLM) scaffolding methods are often validated on narrow, short-horizon tasks like pick-and-place. To deploy robots in unstructured human environments, they must master complex, long-horizon activities (e.g., 'tidy the kitchen') that involve sequencing sub-tasks, managing multiple objectives, and recovering from errors. Pre-2026, there is a critical gap in systematically evaluating and improving learning methods on such diverse and compositionally complex benchmarks.
proposals.hypothesis: A hybrid method that combines multi-modal demonstration data (video and state-action pairs) with a VLM-based task planner and a learned policy refinement module will significantly outperform standard imitation learning baselines on a newly proposed benchmark of long-horizon, multi-objective tasks.
proposals.method: We will first curate a benchmark of 20+ complex robotic tasks, defined by natural language instructions, that require tool use, long sequences, and soft constraints (e.g., 'avoid spilling'). We will collect multi-modal human demonstrations (kinesthetic, VR, and video). Our core method will train a base policy via multi-modal imitation learning (using architectures like Diffusion Policy or ACT). This policy will be integrated with a frozen VLM (e.g., GPT-4V) for high-level step decomposition and monitored by a lightweight, trainable 'refinement critic' network. This critic, trained on demonstration success/failure signals, will learn to override the VLM's plan or adjust the base policy's actions when predicted performance is low.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) a publicly available benchmark for complex, language-instructed robotic manipulation, 2) empirical analysis of how demonstration modality and VLM planning scale with task complexity, and 3) a novel hybrid architecture that robustly combines learned policies with symbolic reasoning for long-horizon tasks.
proposals.novelty: While VLMs for planning and multi-modal imitation are known, their systematic integration and evaluation on a broad spectrum of *compositionally complex* tasks, with a learned module to mitigate VLM planning failures, is a novel direction for robust robot learning.

proposals.title: Inferring Reward Corrections: An IRL Framework for Refining Robot Policies from Sparse Human Feedback
proposals.motivation: Imitation learning from demonstrations is data-inefficient and brittle to distribution shifts, while explicit reward specification for complex tasks is often intractable. Pre-2026, methods for incorporating human feedback, like corrections or preferences, often treat it as direct policy labels or simple reward shaping, failing to infer the underlying human intent for robust policy refinement. This limits the adaptability of robots in long-horizon, multi-stage tasks where human feedback is sparse but critical.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that modeling sparse human corrections and demonstrations through an inverse reinforcement learning (IRL) lens can infer a more accurate and generalizable reward function than direct behavioral cloning, leading to policies that are more robust to novel situations and require fewer expert interventions.
proposals.method: We propose a two-phase framework. First, an initial policy is learned via behavior cloning from a small set of demonstrations. Second, during policy execution, sparse human corrections (e.g., trajectory waypoints, language instructions) are treated as optimality evidence for a Maximum Entropy IRL process (MaxEnt IRL or its deep variants). The inferred reward function will be used to fine-tune the policy via reinforcement learning (e.g., PPO or soft actor-critic). We will integrate a vision-language model (VLM) to parse natural language corrections into a structured feature space for the IRL reward network.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a unified IRL-based framework for learning from both demonstrations and subsequent corrections, 2) empirical evidence on the sample efficiency and robustness gains of IRL-for-refinement over standard imitation learning with corrections, and 3) a publicly available dataset of robot task executions paired with human corrective feedback.
proposals.novelty: While IRL is well-studied and human corrections are used in interactive imitation learning, the novel integration lies in using IRL explicitly to interpret sparse, post-demonstration corrections as reward signals for refinement, moving beyond treating them as mere trajectory overrides or preference comparisons.

proposals.title: Explainable Robot Intent: Inferring and Articulating Reward Functions from Behavioral Traces
proposals.motivation: As robots learn complex tasks from human demonstrations and reinforcement learning, their underlying reward functions become opaque, hindering trust and collaboration. Pre-2026, methods like Inverse Reinforcement Learning (IRL) infer rewards but produce uninterpretable vectors, not human-aligned explanations. This work addresses the critical need for robots to explain *why* they behave as they do by translating learned rewards into natural language, enabling effective human-robot communication and debugging.
proposals.hypothesis: A system that combines multi-modal behavioral analysis with a vision-language model (VLM) can infer a structured representation of a robot's reward function and generate faithful, intuitive natural language explanations of its objectives and preferences.
proposals.method: We will first train an IRL model (e.g., Maximum Entropy IRL) on robot demonstration videos to recover a latent reward vector. This vector will be used to condition a VLM (like CLIP or a VLM fine-tuned on robotics data) that also processes the same visual trajectory. The VLM will be trained via a novel captioning objective to generate textual explanations (e.g., "The robot prioritizes avoiding the red object") that are grounded in the inferred reward. We will validate explanations through human studies measuring correctness and clarity.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel framework for explainable reward inference that bridges the gap between numerical reward functions and human-interpretable concepts, and 2) a benchmark and metrics for evaluating the fidelity and usefulness of robot-generated intent explanations in collaborative tasks.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses VLMs for task planning or scaffolds manipulation, applying them to the *inverse problem* of reward explanation from observed behavior is novel. The key innovation is the joint training of IRL and a language model to produce explanations that are directly tied to the recovered reward structure, not just descriptive captions.

proposals.title: Language-Guided Hierarchical Reward Decomposition for Long-Horizon Robot Learning
proposals.motivation: Scaling robot learning to complex, long-horizon tasks in unstructured environments remains a fundamental challenge. Pre-2026, imitation learning and reinforcement learning (RL) often struggle with sparse rewards and the need for extensive, high-quality demonstrations. While large language models (LLMs) have shown promise in high-level planning, their direct use for low-level control is inefficient. This work addresses the core bottleneck of reward specification for temporally extended tasks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a hierarchical reward structure, automatically decomposed from natural language task descriptions using an LLM, will enable more sample-efficient policy learning for long-horizon robot manipulation compared to flat reward shaping or end-to-end imitation.
proposals.method: We propose a two-stage framework. First, a frozen LLM (e.g., GPT-4) decomposes a high-level language instruction into a temporal graph of sub-task descriptions and generates corresponding dense reward functions using code-as-policy techniques. Second, a hierarchical RL agent (e.g., using HIRO or an option-critic architecture) is trained, where the high-level policy selects sub-tasks and the low-level policy maximizes the LLM-generated sub-task rewards. We will validate the method on simulated and real-robot long-horizon manipulation benchmarks (e.g., MetaWorld, Franka Kitchen).
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, language-in-the-loop paradigm for automatic reward engineering, reducing the need for manual reward design or exhaustive demonstration. It will provide empirical evidence on how semantic task decomposition from LLMs can guide and accelerate hierarchical RL, advancing the sample efficiency of learning for complex robot tasks.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in using an LLM not for direct planning or policy generation, but specifically for the automatic synthesis and structuring of a *hierarchical reward function*, creating a bridge between high-level semantics and low-level reinforcement learning signals that is more refined than prior end-to-end or primitive-based methods.

proposals.title: Temporal Language-Embedded Imitation: Learning Robotic Manipulation from Video Demonstrations with Spatio-Temporal Reasoning
proposals.motivation: Current vision-language models (VLMs) for robot instruction, as explored in prior work, primarily parse static scenes, limiting their applicability to real-world tasks that involve moving objects, occlusions, and dynamic state changes. Enabling robots to understand and reason about spatial relationships over time from video demonstrations is crucial for complex, long-horizon manipulation in unstructured environments. This bridges a critical gap between high-level language-guided planning and low-level control in dynamic settings.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a VLM-based policy, explicitly trained to extract and reason over spatio-temporal graphs from video demonstrations, will significantly outperform static-image-based imitation learning methods on tasks requiring dynamic scene understanding and temporal reasoning.
proposals.method: We will extend the VLM scaffolding framework by integrating a temporal reasoning module. This module will process video demonstrations to construct dynamic scene graphs, where nodes represent objects and edges represent spatial relationships (e.g., 'left of', 'touching') that evolve over time. We will fine-tune a pre-trained VLM (e.g., CLIP or a VLM from 2024-2025) on a curated dataset of video demonstrations paired with language instructions and temporal logic predicates. The policy will be trained using a combination of behavior cloning on the extracted state-action sequences and a multi-agent reinforcement learning objective, inspired by prior social deduction work, to reason about future state transitions.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel method for embedding temporal and spatial reasoning into VLM-based robotic policies, 2) a benchmark dataset of video demonstrations for dynamic manipulation tasks with temporal language annotations, and 3) empirical evidence showing the superiority of video-based dynamic reasoning over static image conditioning for manipulation in non-stationary environments.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses VLMs for static scene parsing or language-conditioned control, our approach uniquely integrates explicit temporal reasoning over dynamic scene graphs into the imitation learning pipeline, moving beyond single-image conditioning to continuous video understanding for robotics.

proposals.title: Spatial Chain-of-Thought: Integrating Vision-Language Models with Hierarchical Policies for Physical Interaction Tasks
proposals.motivation: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have shown promise for high-level task planning in robotics, but their application to complex physical interactions requiring spatial reasoning—like stacking, inserting, or arranging objects under constraints—remains limited. Current methods often treat VLMs as monolithic planners, which struggle with the low-level geometric and dynamic precision needed for successful manipulation. This gap prevents the deployment of flexible, language-instructed robots in unstructured environments like homes or warehouses.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a hierarchical framework, which decomposes a VLM's spatial reasoning into a sequence of actionable geometric sub-goals for a learned low-level policy, will significantly improve the success rate and sample efficiency of language-conditioned physical interaction tasks compared to end-to-end VLM planning.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-tier architecture. The high-level planner will be a fine-tuned VLM (e.g., based on CLIP or BLIP-2) that, given a language instruction and scene image, generates a 'spatial chain-of-thought'—a sequence of intermediate spatial states (e.g., 'grasp cup from top', 'align handle with hook'). This structured output will condition a low-level policy trained via offline imitation learning on human demonstrations of dexterous manipulation, similar to Sadigh's prior work on demonstration modalities. We will integrate the framework using a simulation benchmark (e.g., RLBench) featuring tasks requiring precise spatial alignment and force interaction.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel hierarchical integration method that bridges the semantic reasoning of VLMs with the precision of learned manipulation policies, 2) a public benchmark for evaluating language-guided physical interaction, and 3) empirical evidence on how structured spatial reasoning from language improves task performance and robustness.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses VLMs for task planning or affordance prediction, our approach uniquely leverages them to generate explicit, interpretable spatial reasoning chains as an interface for low-level control, directly addressing the precision gap in physical interaction. This differs from end-to-end methods or those using VLMs only for high-level goal specification.

proposals.title: Active Querying for Spatial Reasoning: Transferring Robotic Active Learning to Vision-Language Model Training
proposals.motivation: Training robust vision-language models (VLMs) for spatial reasoning requires vast, diverse datasets, which are expensive and time-consuming to curate manually. In robotics, active learning algorithms efficiently query the most informative physical interactions for skill acquisition. This project addresses the critical bottleneck of data efficiency in VLM training by investigating whether robotic active learning principles can be transferred to guide automated, intelligent data generation for spatial understanding tasks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an active learning strategy, originally designed for robotic manipulation to query informative physical states, can be adapted to identify and generate the most informative spatial scenarios (e.g., complex object arrangements, occlusions, relational queries) for training VLMs, leading to superior sample efficiency and generalization compared to passive, random data collection.
proposals.method: We will adapt a robotic uncertainty-based active learning algorithm (e.g., Bayesian Deep Learning for predicting task success) to a VLM setting. The method will use a pre-trained VLM (e.g., CLIP or a VLM from the researcher's prior work) as a base model and a spatial scenario simulator (e.g., PyBullet). The algorithm will iteratively: 1) generate candidate spatial scenes, 2) quantify the VLM's predictive uncertainty on language-guided spatial reasoning tasks for each scene, and 3) query the scenes with highest uncertainty for synthetic data generation and fine-tuning. Performance will be evaluated on benchmarks like CLEVR and real-world robotic instruction following.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, sample-efficient framework for generating training data for VLMs, specifically targeting spatial reasoning. It will provide empirical evidence for the transfer of robotic learning principles to the domain of large-scale model training. The resulting actively-trained VLM is expected to show improved robustness and generalization on downstream robotic tasks requiring language-guided spatial understanding.
proposals.novelty: While active learning is well-studied in robotics and for fine-tuning language models, its application to strategically generate multi-modal training data for VLMs—particularly by directly translating robotic query strategies for spatial uncertainty—is a novel and non-obvious direction. This bridges the gap between data-centric AI and embodied learning paradigms.

proposals.title: EdgeVLM: Efficient Vision-Language Models for Real-Time Spatial Reasoning on Robotic Edge Devices
proposals.motivation: Deploying large vision-language models (VLMs) on resource-constrained robotic platforms is infeasible due to high computational and memory demands, limiting real-time interaction and autonomy. Pre-2026, efficient model design focuses on general language tasks, not the core spatial and physical reasoning required for robotics. Enabling performant, small VLMs on edge devices is critical for scalable, responsive, and privacy-preserving human-robot collaboration.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by co-designing a compact VLM architecture with a training curriculum focused on spatial relationships and affordances, we can achieve real-time reasoning performance on edge hardware comparable to larger models for embodied tasks.
proposals.method: We will develop a lightweight, hybrid architecture combining a distilled visual encoder (e.g., from ViT) with a small, task-specific language model. The model will be trained via imitation learning from human demonstrations, using a novel multi-modal dataset of physical interactions annotated with spatial language (e.g., 'left of', 'graspable'). We will employ techniques like quantization-aware training and knowledge distillation from a large teacher VLM (e.g., based on CLIP or GPT-3) to preserve core reasoning capabilities while drastically reducing parameters.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel, publicly available benchmark for evaluating spatial reasoning of efficient VLMs on robotic manipulation tasks, 2) a methodology for creating task-efficient VLMs via demonstration-driven curriculum learning, and 3) open-source models and deployment pipelines for common robotic edge computing platforms (e.g., NVIDIA Jetson).
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work on general VLM compression, our approach explicitly optimizes for the spatial and physical reasoning primitives essential for robotics, using human demonstration data as a grounding signal, which is a departure from scaling down web-trained models directly.

proposals.title: Meta-Adaptation of Multi-Agent Generative Adversarial Imitation Learning Policies for Fast Task Generalization
proposals.motivation: Multi-agent systems, such as collaborative robots or agents in social deduction games, must adapt to new partners and tasks with minimal data. Pre-2026, methods like Multi-Agent Generative Adversarial Imitation Learning (MAGAIL) can learn policies from demonstrations but require extensive retraining for new scenarios. This data inefficiency is a major bottleneck for deploying adaptable multi-agent systems in real-world settings where task specifications frequently change.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a meta-imitation learning framework, which learns a prior over MAGAIL policy parameters from a distribution of related multi-agent tasks, will enable rapid adaptation to new tasks with only a few demonstrations, significantly outperforming standard fine-tuning approaches.
proposals.method: We will extend the Reptile or MAML meta-learning algorithm to operate on the generator network of a MAGAIL architecture. The outer loop will meta-train on a diverse set of source multi-agent tasks (e.g., different coordination games or manipulation scenarios), each with its own demonstration set. The inner loop will perform a few gradient steps using demonstrations from a held-out target task. We will evaluate adaptation speed and final performance on novel multi-agent tasks in simulated environments, benchmarking against standard MAGAIL training from scratch and direct fine-tuning.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a principled framework for data-efficient adaptation of multi-agent imitation learning policies. It will provide empirical evidence on the sample efficiency gains of meta-learning for multi-agent systems and release a benchmark suite of adaptable multi-agent tasks for future research in this direction.
proposals.novelty: While meta-learning and MAGAIL are established separately, their combination for fast adaptation of multi-agent adversarial imitation policies is novel. This approach moves beyond using meta-learning only for single-agent reinforcement learning or behavioral cloning, explicitly addressing the unique non-stationarity challenges in multi-agent imitation.

proposals.title: KnowNo-VLM: Uncertainty-Aware Alignment for Vision-Language Models in Embodied Task Planning
proposals.motivation: Vision-language models (VLMs) are increasingly used for high-level task planning in robotics, but they often generate confident yet incorrect or unsafe plans when faced with ambiguous instructions or novel environments. This overconfidence poses a significant risk for real-world deployment. The KnowNo framework successfully quantified and aligned uncertainty for language-only models, but this critical capability is missing for multimodal models that must interpret visual scenes.
proposals.hypothesis: Extending the KnowNo uncertainty quantification and alignment framework to vision-language models will enable them to reliably express uncertainty over generated plans, allowing them to appropriately seek human clarification and significantly improve task success and safety in embodied settings.
proposals.method: We will adapt the KnowNo framework, which uses conformal prediction to provide statistical guarantees on model uncertainty, for a VLM backbone (e.g., CLIPort or a fine-tuned ViT-LLM). The model will be trained on multimodal datasets of embodied tasks with ambiguous instructions. We will develop a novel conformal scoring function that jointly considers visual scene ambiguity and linguistic instruction uncertainty to produce calibrated prediction sets of possible actions. The system will be integrated into a robot policy that triggers help requests when the uncertainty exceeds a calibrated threshold.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first uncertainty-aware VLM for robotics with statistical guarantees on help-seeking behavior. It will provide a principled method for safe human-robot interaction by preventing overconfident execution. We will release a benchmark for evaluating uncertainty in embodied VLMs and demonstrate improved task success rates in real-robot experiments.
proposals.novelty: While KnowNo addressed language uncertainty and VLMs have been used for planning, no prior work has integrated conformal prediction-based uncertainty quantification specifically for the joint vision-language reasoning required in embodied tasks. This bridges a critical gap between reliable AI alignment and multimodal robot learning.

proposals.title: Robust Long-Horizon Task Execution via Language-Conditioned Imitation Learning with Dynamic Environment Adaptation
proposals.motivation: Current robot imitation learning methods, including those using language scaffolding, are primarily evaluated on short, structured tasks in controlled environments. Real-world deployment, however, requires robots to execute extended sequences of actions (long-horizon) while adapting to unforeseen environmental changes, a critical challenge that remains under-explored. Success in this domain is essential for practical applications like assistive robotics and household automation.
proposals.hypothesis: A framework combining language-conditioned hierarchical policies with a memory-augmented adaptation module, trained on multi-modal demonstrations, will enable robust long-horizon task completion in the face of dynamic environmental perturbations.
proposals.method: We will extend a hierarchical imitation learning architecture where a high-level Vision-Language Model (VLM) planner, similar to those in Sadigh's prior work, decomposes long-horizon tasks into language-specified sub-goals. A low-level policy, trained on multi-modal (vision, proprioception, language) demonstrations, executes these sub-goals. Crucially, we will integrate a differentiable memory module (e.g., a recurrent state estimator or an external memory) that allows the low-level policy to condition its actions on a history of observations, enabling online adaptation to changes like moved objects or altered lighting. Training will use a combination of offline human demonstrations and online fine-tuning with reinforcement learning for robustness.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a benchmark and methodology for evaluating long-horizon, dynamic task performance in robotics. It will yield insights into how language models and memory systems can be combined to create more resilient and generalizable robot policies, moving beyond static demonstration datasets.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses VLMs for planning or multi-modal demos for imitation, the integration of a persistent memory mechanism specifically for in-task adaptation to environmental dynamics within a language-conditioned hierarchy is a novel synthesis for long-horizon robustness.

proposals.title: Learning to Ask: A Reinforcement Learning Framework for Autonomous Help-Seeking in Human-Robot Collaboration
proposals.motivation: In human-robot collaboration, a key challenge is balancing autonomy with appropriate reliance on human assistance. Current methods often rely on fixed thresholds or heuristics for help-seeking, which are brittle and fail to adapt to dynamic human states and task contexts. Developing robots that can learn when to ask for help is crucial for efficient, safe, and user-aligned task execution, especially in unstructured environments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a reinforcement learning agent, trained with a reward function that balances task progress, human effort, and user frustration, can learn a dynamic help-seeking policy that outperforms static threshold-based methods in collaborative task performance and user satisfaction.
proposals.method: We will frame help-seeking as a Partially Observable Markov Decision Process (POMDP), where the robot's state includes its own uncertainty, task progress, and estimated human state (e.g., inferred availability from gaze or posture). We will employ Deep Q-Networks (DQN) or Soft Actor-Critic (SAC) for policy learning. The agent will be trained in simulation using a proxy human model, then fine-tuned via real-world demonstrations and interactive trials, leveraging Sadigh's prior work on demonstration modalities for imitation learning.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, learnable framework for adaptive help-seeking in robotics. It will provide empirical evidence on the trade-offs between autonomy and dependence, and release a benchmark simulation environment for evaluating such policies. The resulting policy aims to reduce unnecessary interruptions while preventing costly failures.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses RL for task execution, its application to a meta-level 'when-to-ask' policy is novel. The approach uniquely integrates inferred human state and long-term cost of interruptions into the RL objective, moving beyond myopic uncertainty-based triggers.

proposals.title: Aligning Robot Policies with Human Intent: A Hybrid Reward Model Combining LLM-Based Task Inference and Sparse Human Feedback
proposals.motivation: Learning from human demonstrations (IL) or feedback (RLHF) is central to aligning robot behavior with human intent, but each approach has limitations. Demonstrations are costly to scale, and RLHF often requires dense, exhaustive feedback. Pre-trained Vision-Language Models (VLMs) offer a rich source of task knowledge and reward shaping, but their outputs may not align perfectly with a specific user's preferences or the nuances of a physical environment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a hybrid reward model, which integrates dense, task-oriented rewards from a VLM/LLM with sparse, corrective human feedback, will enable more data-efficient and robust policy learning for robotic manipulation, outperforming methods that use either signal in isolation.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage reward model for policy training via reinforcement learning. First, a frozen VLM (e.g., CLIP or a VLM fine-tuned on manipulation datasets) will provide a dense reward signal based on image-goal alignment. Second, we will intermittently query a human for binary preference feedback on trajectory segments, using this to train a separate reward model via Bradley-Terry preference modeling. The final reward for policy optimization will be a weighted sum of the VLM reward and the human-preference reward. We will evaluate in simulated and real-world tabletop manipulation tasks, comparing against pure imitation learning, VLM-only reward, and human-preference-only baselines.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel, hybrid reward formulation for robot policy learning that balances scalable pre-trained knowledge with targeted human alignment, 2) an empirical analysis of the synergy between model-based and human-in-the-loop reward signals, and 3) a framework for more sample-efficient and customizable robot learning from limited human input.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses LLMs/VLMs for reward design or human feedback for alignment, the explicit integration and arbitration between a continuous, task-general VLM reward and sparse, preference-specific human feedback for robotic control is a novel and non-trivial combination, moving beyond using either as a standalone reward source.

proposals.title: MetaPrompt: Meta-Learning for Adaptive LLM Reward Shaping in Multi-Agent and Robotic Imitation
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, Vision-Language Models (VLMs) and LLMs are increasingly used as reward functions or policy scaffolds for complex tasks like dexterous manipulation and social deduction. However, their performance is highly sensitive to the prompt's wording, which is typically hand-crafted and static. This creates a brittle, suboptimal training loop, especially in interactive settings where agent capabilities evolve. An automated method to adapt the LLM's guiding prompt based on observed agent performance is needed to improve sample efficiency and final task success.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a meta-learning framework, which treats the LLM reward prompt as a set of learnable parameters, can autonomously adapt the prompt to maximize downstream agent learning progress, leading to more robust and efficient training than fixed prompts.
proposals.method: We propose a bi-level optimization framework. The inner loop trains a target agent (e.g., a robot policy or a social deduction agent) using reinforcement learning or imitation learning, where the reward is generated by a frozen LLM/VLM conditioned on a meta-prompt. The outer loop uses a Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning (MAML) variant to update the embeddings of this meta-prompt. The meta-objective is to maximize the agent's expected performance or learning speed across a distribution of related tasks or training phases. We will test this on benchmarks from dexterous manipulation (e.g., Adroit) and multi-agent social environments.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a generalizable method for dynamically aligning LLM guidance with an agent's learning stage, 2) empirical evidence of improved training efficiency and robustness over static prompting in robot learning and multi-agent scenarios, and 3) a new perspective on LLMs as adaptive components within a learning system, not just static oracles.
proposals.novelty: While meta-learning has been applied to neural network weights and demonstration selection, applying it directly to optimize the textual prompt of a frozen, billion-parameter LLM for real-time reward shaping is a novel direction. This bridges prompt engineering and gradient-based meta-learning in a way not explored in prior imitation or social AI work.

proposals.title: Embodied Data Augmentation: Improving LLM Physical Reasoning with Robot Interaction Traces
proposals.motivation: Large Language Models (LLMs) exhibit impressive linguistic and symbolic reasoning, but their understanding of physical concepts, dynamics, and affordances remains abstract and often brittle, as it is trained primarily on text and static images. This limits their utility in robotics and embodied AI applications where accurate physical intuition is critical. We propose to invert the typical data flow by using robots not just as consumers of LLM knowledge, but as active data collectors to improve the LLMs themselves.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that training LLMs on structured datasets of robot interaction traces—comprising action sequences, multi-view video, and force/torque data paired with natural language descriptions—will significantly improve their performance on physical reasoning benchmarks and their ability to generate feasible plans for robotic tasks.
proposals.method: We will collect a large-scale dataset of robot demonstrations (kinesthetic teaching and teleoperation) for diverse manipulation tasks, instrumented with RGB-D video, proprioceptive data, and contact forces. This data will be annotated automatically with procedural language using templates and a small amount of human verification. We will then fine-tune a pre-trained Vision-Language Model (e.g., Flamingo, BLIP-2) or a pure LLM (e.g., GPT-4, Llama 2) on this multimodal dataset, using a contrastive or next-token prediction objective. The model's improved physical understanding will be evaluated on held-out robot tasks, physical QA datasets (e.g., PHYRE, CLEVRER), and its ability to scaffold robot manipulation plans.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel, publicly available multimodal dataset of robot interaction traces for physical concept learning, 2) a methodology for using embodied data to augment and ground LLMs, and 3) quantitative evidence that such grounding improves performance on both linguistic and embodied reasoning tasks, closing the simulation-to-reality gap for language models.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses LLMs to guide robots, we propose the inverse: using robot embodiment as a data source to train LLMs. This shifts the robot from a downstream application to an upstream data-collection agent for foundational model improvement, a largely unexplored direction pre-2026.

proposals.title: Efficient Robotic Pattern Control via Task-Specific Distillation of Vision-Language Models
proposals.motivation: Large Vision-Language Models (VLMs) show remarkable pattern-completion and instruction-following abilities for high-level robot planning, but their size and latency make them impractical for real-time, low-level control. Conversely, small, efficient models trained via imitation learning lack the robust semantic understanding and generalization of VLMs. Bridging this gap is critical for deploying robots that can perform dexterous, adaptive manipulation in dynamic human environments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the high-level pattern-completion capabilities of large VLMs can be distilled into a family of small, task-specific control policies that retain robust generalization for in-distribution tasks while achieving the efficiency required for real-time reactive control.
proposals.method: We will leverage a pre-trained VLM (e.g., CLIPort or a fine-tuned variant) to generate a diverse dataset of annotated demonstrations, where the VLM provides high-level subgoal patterns and temporal constraints. This dataset will then be used to train a set of compact, modular transformer or diffusion-based policies via behavior cloning and offline reinforcement learning. We will specifically investigate distillation losses that preserve the VLM's cross-modal reasoning and evaluate performance against both pure imitation learning baselines and slower VLM-only planners on benchmark manipulation tasks.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a framework for generating semantically rich, pattern-aware demonstration data using VLMs, 2) a family of efficient, deployable control policies that inherit VLM generalization, and 3) an empirical analysis of the trade-offs between model size, task generality, and real-time performance in robot learning.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses VLMs for high-level planning or skill chaining, our approach uniquely focuses on distilling their pattern-completion essence directly into low-level, real-time control policies, creating a new class of models that are both semantically grounded and computationally tractable.

proposals.title: Learning Emergent Multi-Robot Coordination Patterns via Language Model State Sequence Modeling
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, scaling robot learning to multi-agent settings remains a significant challenge, as traditional imitation or reinforcement learning struggles with the combinatorial complexity of joint state-action spaces. Effective coordination patterns (e.g., passing, covering, converging) are often implicit in demonstrations but difficult to model explicitly. Large Language Models (LLMs) have shown strong sequence modeling and pattern recognition capabilities in other domains, offering a promising but underexplored tool for capturing these complex multi-agent temporal dependencies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by framing the joint state history of a multi-robot team as a structured token sequence, a pre-trained LLM can be fine-tuned to model and generate feasible future joint states, thereby learning and completing emergent coordination patterns from limited demonstration data.
proposals.method: We will collect multi-robot demonstration trajectories of coordination tasks (e.g., collaborative transport, search). Joint states (robot poses, object states) will be encoded into a structured text prompt, forming a temporal sequence. We will fine-tune a pre-trained decoder-only LLM (e.g., GPT-2/3 architecture) on these sequences using a next-token prediction objective. For control, we will use the LLM as a high-level policy: given a partial sequence of past joint states, the model's predicted next joint state will be translated into individual robot goals via a low-level controller. We will compare performance against baselines like centralized multi-agent RL and behavior cloning.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, data-efficient paradigm for learning multi-robot coordination directly from demonstrations, reducing the need for complex multi-agent reward engineering. It will provide empirical evidence on the capability of sequence models to capture physical multi-agent dynamics and offer a new benchmark for evaluating emergent coordination in learned policies.
proposals.novelty: While LLMs have been used for single-agent robot planning and social deduction, applying them as direct temporal models of low-level, continuous multi-robot joint state sequences is novel. This approach moves beyond using LLMs for high-level task specification, instead leveraging their core sequence modeling strength for foundational multi-agent dynamics prediction.

proposals.title: Learning from Imperfect Demonstrators: Quantifying the Necessity and Sufficiency of Human Data for Robot Policy Learning
proposals.motivation: Imitation learning (IL) and learning from demonstrations (LfD) are central to enabling robots to perform complex tasks. However, collecting high-quality, expert human demonstrations is a significant bottleneck, limiting scalability and accessibility. This work investigates whether policies can be learned effectively from more readily available but imperfect data sources, such as non-expert users or suboptimal demonstrations, addressing a key practical challenge in robot learning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that while human demonstration data is necessary for bootstrapping policy learning for certain dexterous or semantically rich tasks, it is not strictly required to be expert-level; robust policies can be learned from imperfect human data when combined with offline reinforcement learning or reward modeling techniques that can extract and refine the underlying task intent.
proposals.method: We will design a controlled study across simulated and real robotic manipulation tasks (e.g., tabletop rearrangement, tool use). We will collect demonstration datasets with varying levels of expertise and optimality. Our core technical approach will be to train policies using a combination of Behavior Cloning (BC) and Conservative Q-Learning (CQL) for offline RL, which is well-suited to learning from suboptimal data. We will also explore using pre-trained Vision-Language Models (VLMs) to generate dense, language-informed reward functions from the imperfect demonstrations, as a scaffold for policy refinement via RL.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a systematic framework for evaluating the quality-robustness trade-off in learning from demonstrations. It will yield practical guidelines for when and how to use non-expert data, potentially democratizing robot programming. The findings will advance methods for learning robust policies from real-world, noisy human interactions.
proposals.novelty: While prior work studies demonstration modality or optimal data, we specifically isolate and quantify the 'necessity' of human data by benchmarking against synthetic or agent-generated demonstrations, and we focus on algorithmic techniques to compensate for imperfection, rather than simply filtering it out.

proposals.title: Hardware-Agnostic Policy Learning: Investigating Data Sufficiency for Generalization on a Single Robot Platform
proposals.motivation: A prevailing assumption in robot learning is that generalization across diverse tasks and environments requires training on data from heterogeneous hardware platforms. This drives costly, multi-robot data collection efforts. However, it remains an open question whether a single, simple, and widely available robot platform (e.g., a fixed-base manipulator with a parallel jaw gripper) could achieve similar generalization if provided with orders of magnitude more diverse, large-scale demonstration data, leveraging advances in scalable imitation and language-conditioned learning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that with a sufficiently large and diverse dataset of demonstrations and language instructions, a policy trained exclusively on a single, simple robot platform can generalize to novel tasks and perceptual environments at a level comparable to policies trained on data aggregated from multiple, morphologically diverse platforms.
proposals.method: We will collect a massive, language-annotated dataset of demonstrations for a broad set of manipulation tasks using only one robot platform (e.g., a Franka Emika Panda). We will train a multi-task, vision-language policy (e.g., a transformer-based architecture like RT-2 or a diffusion policy) on this data. Its generalization performance will be rigorously benchmarked against a strong baseline policy trained on the RoboNet or similar multi-robot dataset, using a held-out suite of novel tasks and adversarial visual environments. We will perform ablation studies scaling the amount of single-platform data to identify performance saturation points.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic evidence for or against the necessity of diverse hardware for generalization in robot learning. It will establish concrete data-scaling laws for single-platform training and offer practical guidance for the research community on resource-efficient data collection strategies. The resulting large-scale single-robot dataset and benchmark will be a valuable open-source resource.
proposals.novelty: While prior work aggregates data from different robots, we explicitly challenge this paradigm by testing the sufficiency of data diversity from a single, simple embodiment. This directly interrogates a core, untested assumption in the field about the sources of generalization.

proposals.title: Beyond Observation: A Taxonomy of Manipulation Skills for Visual-Only Imitation Learning
proposals.motivation: Scaling robot learning requires vast datasets of human demonstrations, but collecting physical interaction data is expensive and constrained. The rise of large-scale human video datasets (e.g., Ego4D, Something-Something) offers a potentially unlimited source of task knowledge. However, it remains unclear which fundamental manipulation skills can be acquired from passive visual observation alone versus those that inherently require physical interaction data, a critical question for efficient and scalable robot training.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a taxonomy of manipulation skills can be defined based on their learnability from visual-only human videos, where tasks requiring fine-grained force modulation or haptic feedback will show a significant performance gap compared to those defined primarily by kinematic trajectories and object affordances.
proposals.method: We will curate a benchmark of manipulation tasks spanning a spectrum from kinematic (e.g., block stacking) to dynamic (e.g., dough kneading). We will train two imitation learning agents per task: one on a large dataset of human videos (using pre-2026 methods like TimeSformer or R3M for feature extraction), and a second on a paired, but much smaller, dataset of physical robot demonstrations. We will employ the same policy architecture (e.g., diffusion policies or action chunking transformers) for both, isolating the effect of the demonstration modality. Performance will be evaluated on a physical robot platform across success rate and task-quality metrics.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) a principled taxonomy categorizing manipulation skills by their dependence on physical interaction data, 2) quantitative evidence defining the limits of visual-only imitation learning, and 3) practical guidance for dataset creation, indicating when costly physical data is necessary versus when scalable video data suffices.
proposals.novelty: While prior work compares demonstration modalities, our approach systematically deconstructs the *task* variable itself to identify the intrinsic properties that dictate the necessity of physical interaction, moving beyond aggregate performance comparisons to a causal, skill-grounded analysis.

proposals.title: Federated Imitation Learning from Heterogeneous Human Demonstrations for Scalable Robot Policy Acquisition
proposals.motivation: Training robust robot policies requires diverse, large-scale data, but centralizing human demonstrations from distributed robots raises significant privacy, bandwidth, and logistical challenges. Existing imitation learning methods, including those exploring demonstration modality, typically assume centralized data collection, limiting scalability and real-world deployment. Federated learning offers a promising paradigm to learn from distributed data while preserving privacy, but its application to robot learning from heterogeneous human demonstrations remains underexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: A federated learning framework for imitation learning can effectively train performant robot policies by aggregating knowledge from distributed, non-IID human demonstrations without raw data exchange, and will outperform locally trained models while matching the performance of centralized training under realistic heterogeneity constraints.
proposals.method: We will adapt a federated averaging (FedAvg) framework to train a shared policy network (e.g., a vision-language model or behavior cloning network) across a population of robots. Each robot performs local policy updates using its unique on-board dataset of human demonstrations (varying in modality—kinesthetic, teleoperation, visual—as per Sadigh's prior work). We will develop and test novel aggregation strategies to handle the non-IID and task-heterogeneous nature of robot data, potentially integrating techniques from meta-learning or personalized federated learning. Evaluation will compare the federated policy's performance on standardized task suites against centralized and locally-trained baselines.
