proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled framework for privacy-preserving, scalable robot learning from human teachers. It will yield new algorithms for federated learning with highly heterogeneous, embodied agents and an empirical analysis of the trade-offs between data privacy, communication cost, and final policy performance in real-world robot learning scenarios.
proposals.novelty: While federated learning is established in other domains, its integration with imitation learning for physical robots, specifically addressing the challenges of multi-modal and non-IID human demonstration data, is a novel direction. This moves beyond prior work that centralizes data or studies modality in isolation.

proposals.title: Augmenting Reality for Robots: Robust Visual and Dynamic Data Augmentation for Generalizable Imitation Learning
proposals.motivation: Imitation learning from human demonstrations is data-inefficient and struggles with generalization to novel visual and dynamic conditions, such as different lighting, object textures, or surface friction. Collecting exhaustive real-world data to cover this variability is prohibitively expensive. While data augmentation is a cornerstone of computer vision for improving model robustness, its application to robot learning—where both visual observations and physical dynamics must be considered—remains underexplored and non-trivial.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a systematic, co-designed framework for visual and dynamic data augmentation applied to robot demonstration datasets will significantly improve the sample efficiency and task generalization of imitation learning policies, without requiring additional real-world demonstrations.
proposals.method: We will develop a pipeline that applies targeted augmentations to both the visual observations and the underlying state-action trajectories in a calibrated, physics-aware manner. For vision, we will use domain randomization techniques (e.g., varying textures, lighting, backgrounds) on demonstration videos. For dynamics, we will apply perturbations to the action sequences or simulated physical parameters (e.g., mass, friction) within stable bounds, using techniques from robust control. We will train policies, such as Behavior Cloning or Diffusion Policies, on this augmented data and evaluate their zero-shot generalization in both simulated and real-robot manipulation tasks.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled methodology for applying computer-vision-style augmentation to the embodied learning setting. It will yield concrete benchmarks showing the impact of different augmentation strategies on imitation learning performance and generalization, moving beyond simple image transformations to holistic trajectory augmentation.
proposals.novelty: While data augmentation is common in vision, its joint application to both the visual and dynamic dimensions of robot demonstration data is novel. The key innovation is the co-design of augmentations that are plausible under physical constraints, rather than applying visual and dynamic perturbations independently.

proposals.title: Learning to Scaffold Long-Horizon Tasks via Hierarchical Language-Conditioned Imitation from Human Demonstrations
proposals.motivation: Enabling robots to perform long-horizon, multi-step tasks (e.g., 'prepare a simple meal') in unstructured environments remains a core challenge in robotics. Pre-2026 methods often evaluate on short, segmented skills or simulated benchmarks, lacking a deep understanding of the failure modes and data scaling laws for real-world, complex sequences. A focused, in-depth study on a single, demanding long-horizon task is needed to identify the bottlenecks in learning from human demonstrations and the role of language for temporal abstraction.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a hierarchical policy, trained on a dense, multi-modal dataset of a single complex task, will outperform flat imitation learning, and that natural language annotations provided by the demonstrator will be critical for learning recoverable sub-task boundaries and enabling high-level task re-planning.
proposals.method: We will collect a large-scale, multi-modal demonstration dataset (kinesthetic, video, and synchronized audio narration) for a single, complex long-horizon task like 'assembling a furniture kit with tools.' We will train and compare two primary architectures: 1) a flat, time-convolutional behavioral cloning (BC) baseline, and 2) a hierarchical model where a high-level Vision-Language Model (VLM) planner, inspired by pre-2026 scaffolding work, outputs language sub-goals for a low-level language-conditioned diffusion policy. We will use offline reinforcement learning (e.g., Conservative Q-Learning) to fine-tune the low-level policy from the demonstration data.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the community with a rich, public benchmark dataset focused on a single complex task, enabling detailed failure analysis. It will yield concrete insights into the sample efficiency and generalization benefits of hierarchical vs. flat imitation for long horizons, and establish best practices for integrating natural language supervision into long-term task learning.
proposals.novelty: While hierarchical learning and language conditioning are known, their synergistic value and precise failure modes for a single, real-world long-horizon task have not been rigorously isolated and studied through targeted, deep-dive data collection and ablation.

proposals.title: Cross-Embodiment Policy Learning via Latent Action Alignment
proposals.motivation: Imitation learning and human-robot interaction typically assume a shared, low-level action space (e.g., joint torques) between demonstrator and learner. This fails for tasks where embodiments differ fundamentally, such as learning a manipulation policy from human video demonstrations for a robot arm, or transferring skills between a wheeled robot and a quadruped. Pre-2026, methods like domain adaptation for state spaces exist, but the core challenge of mismatched, embodiment-specific action representations remains largely unaddressed.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that policies can be learned across disparate embodiments by aligning human and robot actions in a shared, task-oriented latent space, enabling skill transfer without requiring a manually specified, shared low-level action space.
proposals.method: We propose a two-stage framework. First, we train separate variational autoencoders (VAEs) to encode high-dimensional, embodiment-specific actions (e.g., human hand keypoints, robot joint velocities) into latent distributions. Second, we learn a cross-modal alignment module, using contrastive learning or a cycle-consistency loss, to map these latents into a shared, task-relevant latent action space. This aligned space then conditions a policy network (trained via imitation learning from demonstrations) that outputs commands in the learner's own action space, guided by the aligned latent goal.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a formal framework and practical algorithms for cross-embodiment imitation learning, moving beyond state-only alignment. It will enable new human-robot teaching paradigms where the human demonstrates tasks naturally (e.g., via video) for robots with incompatible kinematics, advancing toward more general and accessible robot skill acquisition.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in directly inverting the standard shared action-space assumption; instead of mapping observations, we learn to align the *action representations themselves* across embodiments through a task-grounded latent space, a direction distinct from prior work on visual domain adaptation for imitation.

proposals.title: RT-X-Loco: Scaling Cross-Robot Generalization for Locomotion and Mobile Manipulation via Large-Scale Datasets and Policy Consolidation
proposals.motivation: The success of RT-X (Robotic Transformer X-embodiment) in 2023 demonstrated that consolidating diverse, large-scale datasets from multiple robot platforms can significantly improve generalization for tabletop manipulation. However, the most challenging and impactful robotic tasks involve embodied agents that must navigate and interact with open-world environments, a domain where data scarcity and morphological heterogeneity are even more severe. Extending the X-embodiment paradigm to locomotion and mobile manipulation is critical for developing robots that can operate reliably in human-centric spaces like homes and hospitals.
proposals.hypothesis: Consolidating heterogeneous datasets of locomotion and mobile manipulation trajectories from diverse robot morphologies (e.g., legged, wheeled, tracked) into a single, large-scale training corpus will produce foundation models that exhibit significantly improved zero-shot generalization and sample efficiency on novel mobile tasks and unseen robot platforms, compared to models trained on single-robot or single-domain data.
proposals.method: We will collect and curate a large-scale dataset (RT-X-Loco) comprising teleoperated demonstrations and autonomous task executions from multiple research labs, featuring diverse mobile platforms (Boston Dynamics Spot, Unitree Go1, TurtleBot, etc.) performing navigation, door opening, and object fetch-and-carry tasks. We will adapt the RT-1/RT-2 transformer-based policy architecture to process high-dimensional, multi-modal inputs (RGB, depth, proprioception, lidar) and output both base velocity commands and arm actions. Training will employ a combination of behavior cloning on the consolidated dataset and offline reinforcement learning (using algorithms like Conservative Q-Learning or IQL) to improve robustness and handle the multi-modal action distributions inherent in cross-platform data.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first large-scale, publicly available cross-embodiment dataset for locomotion and mobile manipulation, along with a set of pre-trained foundation models. We will provide a systematic evaluation of generalization across robot morphologies and environments, establishing new benchmarks for mobile robot learning. The project will deliver actionable insights into the challenges of data alignment, representation learning, and policy architecture for heterogeneous mobile agents.
proposals.novelty: While RT-X focused on tabletop arms, this proposal pioneers the application of the cross-embodiment consolidation framework to the dynamic, high-dimensional, and underactuated domain of mobile robots. It directly addresses the unique challenges of aligning data across fundamentally different embodiments (e.g., legs vs. wheels) and grounding language instructions in navigation contexts, which is a significant extension beyond prior work.

proposals.title: Proprio-Language Alignment: Learning a Shared Embedding for Robot Kinesthetic State and Natural Language Instructions
proposals.motivation: Current vision-language-action models for robotics often treat proprioception—the robot's internal sense of its joint positions and velocities—as a secondary, structured input, limiting their ability to ground high-level language instructions directly in low-level physical state. This gap makes it difficult for robots to interpret corrective feedback (e.g., "move your elbow higher") or describe their own physical configuration. Grounding language in proprioception is crucial for more intuitive human-robot communication and instruction-following in contact-rich or visually occluded tasks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying cross-modal contrastive learning, inspired by models like CLIP, to align sequences of proprioceptive states with natural language instructions will create a shared embedding space that enables bidirectional translation between language and physical motion, improving the sample efficiency and generalization of language-conditioned robot policies.
proposals.method: We will train a dual-encoder model where a transformer encoder processes sequences of proprioceptive states (joint angles, velocities) and a text encoder processes language instructions. Using a large dataset of (proprioception trajectory, language description) pairs collected via teleoperation or simulation, we will optimize the model with a contrastive loss (e.g., InfoNCE) to maximize the similarity of matched pairs. This learned embedding will then be used to initialize or condition an imitation learning policy (e.g., BC or Diffusion Policy) for language-guided manipulation, and evaluated on its ability to follow novel instructions and retrieve language for demonstrated motions.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel method for creating a directly aligned proprioception-language representation, 2) empirical evidence that such alignment improves the generalization and interpretability of language-conditioned policies, and 3) a public dataset and model for proprio-language alignment to facilitate future research in grounded physical reasoning.
proposals.novelty: While CLIP-style alignment is established for vision-language, its application to align raw, time-series proprioceptive data with language is novel, moving beyond the common paradigm of using language to condition policies on top of separate state representations.

proposals.title: Diffusion Policies for Multimodal and Robust Robot Action Generation from Language and Demonstrations
proposals.motivation: Current robot imitation learning methods, such as behavior cloning, often learn unimodal action distributions, leading to brittle performance in the face of perceptual ambiguity or multiple viable action sequences. Diffusion probabilistic models have shown remarkable success in generative modeling for images and text, offering a principled way to capture complex, multimodal distributions. Applying these models to robot action generation could significantly improve robustness and adaptability in human-robot interaction and long-horizon tasks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a diffusion-based policy, trained on a combination of language-conditioned human demonstrations, will generate more diverse and robust robot action sequences compared to standard deterministic or variational autoencoder-based policies, especially in tasks with multiple solution paths or ambiguous language instructions.
proposals.method: We will develop a conditional diffusion policy where the denoising process is guided by a language instruction and an initial visual observation. The model architecture will be based on a temporal U-Net that operates on action sequences. We will train the model on datasets of language-annotated robot demonstrations (e.g., from RLBench or real-world teleoperation). Training will involve a standard diffusion loss to denoise action trajectories, with conditioning features extracted by a pre-trained vision-language model (e.g., CLIP) frozen during policy training. We will benchmark against state-of-the-art behavior cloning and conditional variational autoencoder baselines.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel diffusion-based architecture for language-conditioned robot policy learning, 2) empirical evidence on the benefits of diffusion models for capturing action multimodality in imitation learning, and 3) a publicly released benchmark for evaluating multimodal action generation in language-guided robotics.
proposals.novelty: While diffusion models have been explored for trajectory generation in planning, their integration as a direct, end-to-end language-conditioned *policy* for robot control, particularly within Sadigh's established framework of learning from human demonstrations and language, is a novel synthesis of these pre-2026 research threads.

proposals.title: Efficient Robot Policy Distillation: From RT-X to Real-Time On-Device Control
proposals.motivation: Large-scale multi-robot datasets like RT-X have enabled powerful foundation models for robotic control, but their size and computational demands make real-time deployment on physical robots with limited compute challenging. This creates a critical gap between high-level policy learning and practical, low-latency execution. Efficient, specialized models are needed to bring the broad capabilities of RT-X to affordable, widely deployable robot platforms.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a combination of modality-specific knowledge distillation and structured pruning, guided by robot embodiment and task structure, can produce compact policies from RT-X that retain robust generalization for real-time control while drastically reducing computational footprint.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage distillation pipeline. First, we will use task-aware demonstration data to train a lightweight student model (e.g., a small Transformer or CNN-LSTM hybrid) via behavioral cloning and reinforcement learning, using the frozen RT-X model as a teacher to provide auxiliary supervision and action distribution targets. Second, we will apply iterative magnitude pruning and quantization-aware training, informed by the robot's specific action space and sensor modalities (e.g., prioritizing visual processing pathways for manipulation tasks), to further compress the model for efficient on-robot inference.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a framework for creating embodied, efficient robot policies from large foundation models, 2) empirical benchmarks comparing the performance-efficiency trade-offs of different distillation and pruning techniques for real-time control, and 3) open-source, deployable model checkpoints for common robot platforms derived from RT-X.
proposals.novelty: While model distillation is well-studied in vision and language, its application for compressing embodied, multi-modal robot policies from datasets like RT-X is novel, particularly when tightly coupling the compression strategy with the physical constraints and action semantics of the deployment robot.

proposals.title: Embodiment Inference from Observation: Learning Robot Morphology and Dynamics from Behavioral Traces
proposals.motivation: As robots become more diverse and operate in human environments, understanding their physical capabilities is crucial for effective collaboration and task allocation. Current methods require explicit knowledge of a robot's embodiment, which is often unavailable or incomplete when observing a novel agent. This work addresses the inverse problem: can we infer a robot's physical form and constraints solely from its observed behavior, enabling more adaptable human-robot and robot-robot teams?
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a robot's embodiment—including its degrees of freedom, kinematic structure, and dynamic limits—can be inferred from a sufficiently rich dataset of its task executions, by learning a mapping from observed action trajectories and environmental effects to a latent representation of physical capabilities.
proposals.method: We will collect a large-scale dataset of simulated robot demonstrations across varied embodiments (e.g., robotic arms, mobile manipulators) performing tasks from prior work. We propose a two-stage neural architecture: a temporal encoder (e.g., Transformer or LSTM) processes observed state-action trajectories, and a contrastive learning objective trains this encoder to discriminate between different underlying embodiments. The model will be trained to predict a structured latent vector representing key embodiment parameters (e.g., joint limits, link lengths, base mobility) and validated on its ability to generalize to unseen robot morphologies in simulation and on limited real-world data.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel formulation and benchmark for the embodiment inference problem, 2) a learned model that can predict physical capabilities from behavior, reducing the need for explicit specification, and 3) insights into which behavioral features are most informative for discerning embodiment, advancing theory in robot representation learning.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses demonstrations to learn policies for a known robot, we invert this paradigm to infer the unknown robot itself. This moves beyond classifying known robot types to estimating continuous physical parameters from unstructured observations, a capability not explored in pre-2026 imitation or representation learning literature.

proposals.title: DUAL: Learning a Shared, Embodiment-Agnostic World Model for Cross-Robot Task Planning
proposals.motivation: Current robot learning methods, such as imitation learning from demonstrations, often train policies tightly coupled to a specific robot's embodiment (e.g., a particular arm's kinematics). This limits generalization and data efficiency, requiring extensive retraining for each new platform. A fundamental, shared understanding of physical dynamics and task semantics, decoupled from low-level control, is needed for scalable and adaptable robotic intelligence.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a world model trained on multi-embodiment interaction data can learn a shared, abstract representation of dynamics and affordances, enabling effective task planning via model-based reasoning for robots unseen during training.
proposals.method: We will train a transformer-based world model on a diverse dataset of robot demonstrations (vision, proprioception, language instructions) from different embodiments (e.g., Franka, UR5, mobile manipulators). The model will use modality-specific encoders and a shared latent space, predicting next-state embeddings and task-relevant rewards. Planning will be performed in this latent space using algorithms like Cross-Entropy Method (CEM) or learned latent planners, with the resulting abstract plan decoded into embodiment-specific motor commands via lightweight adapter networks.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a framework for learning embodiment-agnostic world models from heterogeneous robot data, 2) empirical evidence of improved sample efficiency and zero-shot generalization to novel robots compared to policy-centric methods, and 3) a bridge between high-level vision-language reasoning and low-level control, extending the scaffolding concept from prior VLM work.
proposals.novelty: While prior work in multi-task or multi-modal learning shares components, explicitly learning a shared dynamics model for cross-embodiment planning and control, decoupled from policy distillation, is a novel direction. It moves beyond using VLMs for high-level guidance to learning a actionable, generalizable model of physical interaction.

proposals.title: Joint Visual-Language Representation Learning for Task-Specific Robot Manipulation
proposals.motivation: Current robot learning methods that use Vision-Language Models (VLMs) typically rely on large, frozen, pre-trained visual encoders (e.g., CLIP) to provide general-purpose visual features. While effective for broad generalization, these features may be suboptimal for specific, fine-grained manipulation tasks where task-relevant visual details (e.g., object texture, precise pose, or subtle affordances) are crucial. This paradigm limits performance and data efficiency in targeted domains.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that training a visual encoder from scratch, jointly with a language model, on task-specific robot demonstration data will yield visual representations that are more aligned with the manipulation task, leading to superior imitation learning performance compared to using frozen, generic visual features.
proposals.method: We will develop an end-to-end imitation learning framework where a convolutional visual encoder and a transformer-based language model are co-trained via behavioral cloning on paired (image, language instruction, action) trajectories. The model will be trained on a curated dataset of human demonstrations for dexterous manipulation tasks. We will compare against strong baselines using frozen CLIP features, evaluating on metrics of task success rate, robustness to visual distractors, and sample efficiency. Ablation studies will analyze the emergent visual features.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide empirical evidence on the trade-offs between generic pre-training and task-specific joint training for robot learning. It will deliver a new method for learning tightly coupled visuo-linguistic representations from demonstration data, potentially setting a new standard for data-efficient policy learning in visually complex manipulation domains.
proposals.novelty: This inverts the dominant frozen visual encoder paradigm in robot learning with VLMs. While prior work scaffolds manipulation with frozen VLMs, jointly training the visual backbone for a specific robotic task from scratch is a significant and underexplored departure aimed at maximizing task-specific alignment.

proposals.title: Spatial Language as Action: Implicit Fine-Grained Reasoning for Robot Manipulation Without Explicit Localization
proposals.motivation: Current robotic systems for manipulation often rely on explicit localization modules (e.g., bounding box detectors) to ground language instructions, creating a brittle and computationally expensive pipeline. This decoupling of perception from language limits generalization to novel objects and scenes. A unified approach that achieves spatial reasoning purely through language generation could lead to more flexible and interpretable human-robot interaction.
proposals.hypothesis: A vision-language model (VLM) fine-tuned to generate dense, sequential natural language descriptions of spatial relationships and actions can guide a robot's manipulation policy effectively, eliminating the need for a separate, explicit object localization task.
proposals.method: We will build upon pre-trained VLMs (e.g., CLIP-based architectures) and fine-tune them using a novel multi-stage imitation learning objective. The model will be trained on paired demonstrations of manipulation tasks and corresponding dense, step-by-step language narrations of the scene and action (e.g., 'move the gripper left of the red block, then down until it touches the table near the spoon'). This language output will serve as an intermediate representation for training a downstream low-level control policy via reinforcement learning, using the language sequence as a dynamic goal-conditioning signal.
proposals.contribution: This work will demonstrate a new paradigm for robotic spatial reasoning that is more aligned with human communication, reducing system complexity. It will provide a benchmark for evaluating implicit spatial understanding in VLMs for robotics. The resulting models will be more interpretable, as the robot's spatial reasoning is expressed in natural language.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses language for high-level task planning or uses VLMs for affordance prediction, this proposal uniquely frames fine-grained spatial reasoning *itself* as a language generation task, directly replacing coordinate or bounding box outputs with descriptive text to guide low-level control.

proposals.title: Co-Evolving Task Generation and Adversarial Evaluation for Robust Embodied Reasoning
proposals.motivation: Current benchmarks for embodied AI and complex reasoning tasks are static, leading to overfitting and poor generalization to novel, real-world scenarios. This is critical for deploying robots and agents in human environments, where tasks are diverse and failure modes are unpredictable. A dynamic evaluation framework is needed to continuously stress-test and improve the robustness of learning systems.
proposals.hypothesis: A co-evolutionary framework, where a generative model creates diverse reasoning and planning tasks and an adversarial solver actively seeks brittle shortcuts and failure modes, will produce a self-improving benchmark that drives the development of more robust and generalizable embodied AI agents.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-agent system. A task generator, built on a large vision-language model (VLM) like GPT-4V or PaLM-E, will synthesize novel, parameterized embodied tasks (e.g., 'rearrange the kitchen to be safe for a toddler') with procedural validity checks. An adversarial solver, an agent trained via multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) or imitation learning from diverse demonstrations, will attempt to solve these tasks while also being rewarded for finding edge-case failures or policy shortcuts. The failures identified by the adversary will be used as negative examples to retrain the generator, creating an automated feedback loop.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, automated benchmarking system that generates an unbounded stream of valid, complex tasks for embodied AI, moving beyond static datasets. It will provide a formalized method for quantifying and improving adversarial robustness in task planning and physical reasoning. The resulting framework and co-evolved task-solver pairs will be released as a tool for the community.
proposals.novelty: While adversarial training and generative models exist, their integration into a co-evolutionary loop for automated benchmark creation in embodied reasoning is novel. This approach moves beyond fixed human-designed benchmarks or simple data augmentation, creating a live system where task complexity and solver robustness push each other to new limits.

proposals.title: Bayley Scales for Bots: A Developmental Benchmark for Graded Evaluation of Embodied Physical Intelligence
proposals.motivation: Current benchmarks for embodied AI, such as MetaWorld or BEHAVIOR, often present monolithic, end-to-end tasks that obscure which fundamental physical reasoning capabilities an agent lacks. This mirrors the limitations of evaluating human intelligence with a single IQ test, rather than assessing developmental milestones. A structured, hierarchical evaluation suite is needed to diagnose specific weaknesses in physical competence, track progress more interpretably, and guide the development of more capable and generalizable robotic systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that decomposing complex embodied reasoning into a standardized progression of sub-tasks, analogous to infant developmental scales, will yield a more sensitive, interpretable, and predictive benchmark for embodied AI than existing monolithic task evaluations.
proposals.method: We will design a hierarchical benchmark suite in simulation (e.g., Isaac Gym) with tasks grouped into 'stages' of increasing complexity, from simple object affordance recognition and force modulation, to multi-step tool use, and finally to collaborative tasks requiring communication. We will evaluate a spectrum of pre-2026 agents, including imitation learning models (e.g., BC-Z), reinforcement learning policies, and vision-language action models (VLAs) like RT-2, on this suite. Performance will be analyzed via a composite 'Physical Competence Quotient' (PCQ) score and detailed stage-wise diagnostics.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the community with a standardized, open-source benchmark for diagnosing embodied AI capabilities, moving beyond success/failure metrics to a nuanced developmental profile. It will establish a common framework for tracking progress in physical intelligence and reveal which training paradigms (e.g., demonstration modality, language scaffolding) best transfer across competence stages.
proposals.novelty: While prior work creates task collections, the key novelty is the explicit structuring of tasks into a psychologically-inspired developmental hierarchy, enabling a graded, diagnostic evaluation of an agent's 'physical intelligence' akin to standardized human assessments.

proposals.title: Benchmarking Compositional Reasoning: An Extended Evaluation Suite for Language-Guided Robotic Planning
proposals.motivation: Current evaluation suites for language-conditioned robot learning, such as Language-Table or RLBench, focus on short-horizon, single-skill tasks. This fails to capture the compositional reasoning and long-term planning required for real-world deployment, where robots must decompose high-level instructions into sequences of feasible actions. A standardized benchmark for complex, multi-step tasks is needed to rigorously assess progress in embodied AI and human-robot collaboration.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that extending evaluation suites to include complex, multi-step planning tasks will reveal significant performance gaps in current language-conditioned policies and provide a more accurate measure of progress towards generalizable robot learning.
proposals.method: We will design and implement a new benchmark suite within a simulated environment (e.g., PyBullet or iGibson). The suite will feature tasks requiring long-horizon planning, object composition, and spatial reasoning, such as "set a dinner table" or "assemble a toy from parts." We will evaluate a spectrum of pre-2026 methods, including end-to-end imitation learning from demonstrations, modular neuro-symbolic planners, and vision-language model (VLM) prompting for skill chaining, using metrics for task success, plan length optimality, and human-interpretability of failures.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the community with a standardized, open-source benchmark for complex robotic planning, enabling systematic comparison of algorithms. It will establish new performance baselines and identify critical failure modes in long-horizon reasoning, guiding future research in language-guided robotics.
proposals.novelty: While prior work creates benchmarks for isolated skills, this proposal systematically constructs a suite focused on the compositional complexity of tasks, explicitly evaluating how well models can *plan* by grounding and sequencing language instructions, not just execute them.

proposals.title: AdaVLM: Adaptive Vision-Language Co-Training via Learned Granular Learning Rate Modulation
proposals.motivation: Current Vision-Language Model (VLM) training paradigms, such as fine-tuning with frozen visual encoders or full end-to-end training, represent a brittle trade-off. Frozen encoders limit adaptation to novel tasks, while full fine-tuning is computationally expensive and risks catastrophic forgetting or overfitting, especially with limited robot demonstration data. A principled, adaptive method for modulating component plasticity during training is needed to optimize for both parameter efficiency and downstream task performance.
proposals.hypothesis: A lightweight, learned controller that dynamically adjusts per-layer or per-module learning rates based on real-time alignment and utility signals will enable more parameter-efficient and higher-performing VLM adaptation than static training strategies.
proposals.method: We propose a meta-learning framework where a small controller network (e.g., an MLP or LSTM) takes as input layer-wise gradient statistics, feature alignment metrics between vision and language streams, and task loss trends. It outputs a multiplicative learning rate scale for each component (e.g., ViT blocks, LLM layers, cross-attention modules) at each training iteration. This controller is trained via a bi-level optimization loop, where its parameters are updated to minimize a validation loss that balances task performance and a regularization term penalizing excessive parameter change. We will implement and test this on VLM architectures like BLIP-2 and OpenFlamingo, using robotic manipulation and VQA benchmarks.
proposals.contribution: This work will yield 1) a novel, general framework for dynamic vision-language co-adaptation, 2) empirical principles for when and how much to adapt different VLM components, and 3) open-source code for adaptive fine-tuning that improves sample efficiency and final performance on robotic task learning from language and demonstrations.
proposals.novelty: While prior work explores adapter modules or selective fine-tuning, our approach is novel in its fully dynamic, learned granular control over learning rates at the module level during training, framed as a continuous meta-optimization problem rather than a pre-defined architectural or scheduling choice.

proposals.title: Learning to See from Language: A Multi-Modal Imitation Framework for Language-Conditioned Image Generation and Editing
proposals.motivation: Current vision-language models (VLMs) excel at understanding scenes from images and text, but the inverse problem—generating or modifying images from complex, multi-step language instructions—remains a significant challenge for robotics and human-AI interaction. Enabling robots to interpret high-level task descriptions (e.g., 'rearrange the table for a formal dinner') and predict or generate the required visual state is crucial for planning and verifying task execution. This capability would bridge the gap between language-based task specification and visual goal understanding, directly supporting learning from human demonstrations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a model trained on paired sequences of language instructions and visual demonstrations of state changes can learn a generative visual prior, enabling it to produce or edit images that represent the outcome of executing those instructions.
proposals.method: We will leverage a transformer-based architecture similar to those used for instruction-following, but invert the input-output modality. The model will take as input a starting image (or noise) and a language instruction, and output a target image. We will train it using a dataset of human demonstrations for tabletop tasks, where each demonstration is a tuple (initial image, language instruction, final image). Training will combine a reconstruction loss (e.g., diffusion model objective or VQ-GAN) with a contrastive loss aligning the generated image's CLIP embedding with the instruction embedding, ensuring semantic fidelity.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel method for translating procedural language into visual outcomes, providing a 'visual imagination' module for robotic systems. It will advance the field of vision-language robotics by enabling the synthesis of subgoals and failure states from language, which can scaffold reinforcement learning and improve the interpretability of robot plans.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses VLMs for scene understanding or text-to-image generation in open domains, our approach uniquely frames the problem as *visual imitation from language*, directly leveraging robot demonstration datasets and focusing on the precise, instruction-conditioned transformation of existing visual scenes, rather than generation from scratch.

ideas.title: Adapting the 'Turing Test' to a 'Physical Competence Test' for Embodied AI
ideas.description: The original idea proposes expanding AI evaluation to complex embodied tasks like robotic planning. Drawing an analogy from Cognitive Psychology, where complex human development is assessed via standardized milestone tests (e.g., Bayley Scales), the adapted solution is to create a 'Physical Competence Test' for AI. This involves developing a hierarchical evaluation suite that decomposes complex embodied reasoning into a progression of standardized, measurable sub-tasks of increasing difficulty—mirroring developmental stages from simple object interaction to multi-step planning and collaboration. This approach provides a structured, scalable, and interpretable benchmark to track progress in embodied AI systems, moving beyond single, monolithic task evaluations to a graded spectrum of physical intelligence.

ideas.description: This research develops a dynamic evaluation framework where a generative model synthesizes novel, complex reasoning and embodied planning tasks, ensuring a continuous stream of diverse and valid challenges. A separate adversarial model actively attempts to solve these tasks in unintended, brittle ways or to identify edge-case failures, feeding these weaknesses back into the task generation process. This creates a co-evolutionary arms race between task complexity and solver robustness, preventing benchmark overfitting. The output is not a fixed suite but a live, automated system for measuring and driving progress in generalization and adversarial resilience for complex AI tasks.

ideas.title: Dynamic Granular Co-Adaptation for Vision-Language Model Training
ideas.description: This research proposes a framework for dynamically co-training vision and language components in VLMs, replacing the simplistic choice between fully frozen or fully end-to-end training. A lightweight controller network monitors alignment and task-specific utility signals to adaptively modulate per-layer or per-module learning rates throughout training. This allows the model to learn when and how much to adapt each component, optimizing for both parameter efficiency and final task performance. The approach is evaluated across multiple VLM architectures and benchmarks to derive general principles for effective vision-language co-adaptation.

ideas.title: Temporal Fusion Architectures for Sequential Visual Reasoning
ideas.description: This research challenges the standard VLM assumption of single-image input by designing models specifically for sequential visual reasoning (long video or multi-image sequences). Drawing an analogy from seismology, where 'waveform inversion' solves the problem of reconstructing 3D subsurface models from sparse, sequential 2D seismic measurements, we adapt this core insight. The analogous solution treats sequential data not as independent snapshots but as temporal constraints on a continuous underlying model, using physical consistency to fill gaps and infer coherent structure. The adapted idea proposes a new VLM architecture that inverts a sequence of frames into a latent representation of a continuous visual process. This is achieved by building in learned temporal-dynamic constraints, allowing the model to reason about the scene as an evolving whole rather than a disconnected series of images, significantly improving performance on tasks requiring understanding of action, causality, and state progression over time.

It features a hierarchical controller with a learned router that predicts efficient model chains and a fusion module that integrates partial outputs. By pre-computing optimal compositions for common task patterns, caching intermediate results, and employing speculative parallel execution, it maintains the flexibility and specialization of heterogeneous models while achieving latency comparable to a single large model for end-to-end inference.

ideas.title: Interpretable Composition of Heterogeneous Model Ensembles
ideas.description: This research direction develops a framework for dynamically composing small, specialized AI models into a cohesive system, while simultaneously applying neuroscience-inspired interpretability tools—like neural recording and ablation—to the compositional process itself. The synergy allows us to treat the ensemble's interaction patterns (e.g., routing decisions, attention between models, combined activations) as a 'meta-network' that can be instrumented and analyzed. This enables causal understanding of how and why specific sub-models are recruited for a task, diagnosing failures in the compositional logic, and optimizing the system for both performance and transparency. The result is a heterogeneous AI system whose emergent, task-specific behavior is interpretable and steerable in real time.

ideas.title: Curriculum-Driven Visual Encoder Co-Training with General Representation Anchoring
ideas.description: This approach mitigates the data inefficiency and over-specialization of pure joint training from scratch. It begins by pre-training the visual encoder alone using a masked autoencoding objective on a diverse set of images, establishing a robust visual foundation. Subsequently, the visual encoder and language model are co-adapted for the target task through joint fine-tuning. A key innovation is an anchoring loss applied to the visual encoder's outputs, ensuring they remain consistent with its general-purpose pre-trained state, which prevents catastrophic forgetting and maintains out-of-distribution robustness.

ideas.title: Benchmarking and Stress-Testing Non-Finetuning Adaptation Methods
ideas.description: This research direction develops specialized evaluation benchmarks to systematically assess the robustness and characterize the failure modes of non-finetuning adaptation methods (e.g., prompting, in-context learning, adapter modules, or control vectors). The benchmarks will be designed to stress-test these methods under distribution shifts, adversarial inputs, and edge cases to reveal how adaptation stability degrades. This enables a direct comparison of adaptation strategies not just on task performance, but on safety, reliability, and preservation of the base model's knowledge. The ultimate goal is to guide the development of adaptation techniques that are both high-performing and provably robust, moving beyond fine-tuning as the default.

ideas.title: Architecture-Agnostic Adaptation via Modular Skill Libraries
ideas.description: This research proposes developing a diverse ecosystem of specialized, architecturally distinct models, each optimized for a specific skill or reasoning type (e.g., symbolic, causal, multimodal). Instead of adapting a single monolithic model via fine-tuning, a novel, lightweight adaptation controller is developed to dynamically compose these heterogeneous model outputs. This controller uses non-finetuning methods—such as advanced prompting, routing, and inference-time algorithms—to orchestrate task execution without modifying the core parameters of any constituent model. The result is a resilient system that mitigates architectural homogenization risks by leveraging diversity, while achieving flexible adaptation without the fragility and lock-in of traditional transfer learning on a single foundation.

ideas.title: A Phase Diagram Approach to VLM Data Mixtures
ideas.description: This research adapts the phase diagram methodology from materials science to systematically analyze data mixing strategies for Vision-Language Models (VLMs). In metallurgy, phase diagrams predict an alloy's properties based on its elemental composition and processing history. Analogously, we propose constructing 'VLM capability phase diagrams' where the axes represent the compositional mix of pretraining and instruction-tuning data sources, and contours map to resulting performance on a multi-dimensional capability battery (e.g., reasoning, alignment, robustness). By rigorously training models across a grid of mixture ratios and training curricula, the study aims to identify optimal data 'alloys' for specific capability targets, uncover nonlinear interactions and phase transitions between data types, and provide a predictive framework for designing more efficient and effective multimodal training data pipelines.

ideas.title: Extracting Spatial Training Data via Language-Only Probing
ideas.description: This research investigates whether a foundation model's internal representations, trained on web-scale data, can be probed to extract detailed spatial and compositional information about its training examples using purely language-based queries. Instead of attempting to reconstruct raw data pixels or exact text, the method focuses on forcing the model to generate verbose, coordinate-free descriptions that reveal the precise arrangement, relative positions, and attributes of objects within scenes it memorized. This bypasses the need for the model to have an explicit localization head, turning its strong spatial reasoning capability, emergent from next-token prediction, into a tool for data inference. The outcome is a new assessment of privacy risks, showing that models can leak structured scene information previously thought protected by the absence of explicit geometric output tasks.

ideas.title: Generative Task Synthesis for Systematic Emergent Capability Discovery
ideas.description: This research designs an automated system that proactively generates novel evaluation tasks to systematically probe for emergent capabilities. A meta-reasoning module synthesizes new multi-modal challenges by composition, negation, and domain transfer of concepts from established benchmarks like VQA and localization. These synthesized tasks are then formalized into executable evaluations with ground truth, enabling large-scale, objective discovery of model behaviors not captured by static benchmarks. The approach moves beyond passive critique to active, replicable exploration of the model's latent reasoning capacities.

ideas.title: Resilience-Aware Training for Vision-Language Models: Quantifying Homogenization and Promoting Functional Diversity
ideas.description: This research proposes a novel framework that applies ecological resilience theory to the architectural design and training of Vision-Language Models (VLMs). It posits that end-to-end training versus using frozen components leads to different 'functional trait profiles' in the model's representations, which can be quantified using ecological metrics like functional diversity, redundancy, and response diversity. The core investigation is to model how these training choices affect the VLM's resilience to distribution shifts and adversarial perturbations, treating homogenization of learned features as a key systemic risk. The outcome will be a set of resilience-aware training principles and diagnostics that guide the development of more robust, adaptable, and functionally diverse multimodal AI systems.

ideas.title: Architectures for Systematic Visual Relationship Encoding: Benchmark-Driven Design
ideas.description: This research first constructs a diagnostic benchmark that exposes systematic failures of standard visual encoders (ViT, CLIP) in understanding visual relationships, attributes, and compositionality. Using this benchmark as a fixed target, the project then explores novel encoder architectures that incorporate explicit mechanisms for modeling spatial and semantic interactions between objects, such as recurrent attention over entity tokens or graph-based visual feature refinement. The goal is to produce an encoder whose architectural novelty is justified by a measurable and significant improvement on this specific, challenging capability, providing a clear advancement over simply exploring generic alternatives to existing models.

ideas.title: Neuroscience-Guided Pruning for Interpretable, Edge-Optimized Foundation Models
ideas.description: This research direction applies neuroscience-inspired interpretability methods as the primary driver for creating ultra-efficient foundation models. By using techniques like ablation analysis and high-dimensional neural recording on large models, we identify sparse, causally important sub-networks and human-interpretable features that are essential for core task performance. This 'interpretability map' then directly informs a structured, knowledge-preserving pruning and distillation process, sculpting a minimal, edge-deployable model that inherently maintains explainable internal representations. The result is a new class of efficient models whose architecture and behavior are co-designed for both performance on-device and transparency, moving beyond black-box compression.

ideas.title: Robust Specialization via a Gated Generalist-Specialist Architecture
ideas.description: This research investigates a hybrid architecture to overcome the brittleness of pure narrow specialization. It employs a small, frozen generalist foundation model to provide stable, common-sense representations. A separate, larger specialist model is deeply trained on a high-quality narrow corpus. A lightweight, adversarially trained routing network dynamically gates the flow of information based on input, blending the outputs of the two networks to optimize for both in-domain expertise and out-of-domain robustness. The system is evaluated on both core task performance and a battery of edge-case and distribution-shift benchmarks.

ideas.title: Adaptive Interface Layers for Foundation Models
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that fine-tuning is the primary method for model adaptation by drawing an analogy from immunology. In vaccinology, adjuvants are used to enhance and direct the immune system's response to an unchanged antigen, rather than modifying the antigen itself. Adapted to machine learning, this suggests developing 'model adjuvants'—lightweight, trainable interface layers that are inserted before, after, or between the frozen layers of a foundation model. These interfaces would intercept activations, apply learned transformations (e.g., sparse re-weighting, projection, or gating), and redirect information flow to specialize the model for new tasks or domains. The core model remains a fixed, immutable 'antigen,' while the adaptable 'adjuvant' interface learns to optimize the interaction for specific downstream challenges, enabling efficient adaptation without altering foundational knowledge or risking catastrophic forgetting.

ideas.title: Adapting Hierarchical Feature Integration from Computational Biology for Visual Encoders
ideas.description: This research seed proposes investigating novel visual encoder architectures for VLMs by drawing an analogy from computational biology, specifically the breakthrough in protein structure prediction by AlphaFold2. The analogous problem is integrating disparate, hierarchical biological signals (sequence alignments and residue-residue distances) into a coherent 3D structure. AlphaFold2's Evoformer solved this through a structured, iterative attention mechanism where different representation streams co-evolve. Adapted to computer vision, the idea is to design a visual encoder that abandons the flat patch-sequence paradigm. Instead, it would construct a multi-scale graph from an image, with nodes representing visual primitives at different levels of abstraction. A dedicated dual-stream attention module would then allow a local feature stream and a global semantic stream to iteratively refine each other, enabling more efficient and structured integration of visual information for superior VLM performance, moving beyond the architectures of ViT and CLIP.

ideas.title: Specialized Pretraining via Ecological Niche Adaptation
ideas.description: This idea inverts the assumption of broad, general pretraining by investigating if deep specialization on narrow, high-quality data yields superior downstream task performance. Drawing an analogy from evolutionary biology and ecology, where specialist species outcompete generalists within stable, resource-rich niches by evolving highly optimized traits, we adapt this solution to machine learning. The adapted research idea proposes creating a suite of specialist models, each pretrained exclusively on a narrow, high-quality 'ecological niche' of data (e.g., a specific scientific domain, a particular writing style, or a coherent task family). The pretraining protocol is designed to maximize performance and parameter efficiency within that specific niche, much like evolutionary adaptation. The hypothesis is that such niche-adapted specialist models will demonstrate superior performance on downstream tasks within their domain of specialization compared to a broadly pretrained generalist model of equivalent scale, challenging the prevailing 'scaling law' paradigm that favors ever-larger, general corpora.

ideas.title: Inverse Scaling Laws for Visual Encoder Design
ideas.description: This research treats the design of novel visual encoder architectures as an inverse problem to model scaling. Instead of scaling up a known architecture and then compressing it, we start from the performance and efficiency targets of a compressed, deployment-ready model and mathematically derive the optimal architectural priors and scaling pathways to reach them. This approach will generate new encoder families whose components (e.g., attention mechanisms, spatial hierarchies, tokenization) are intrinsically aligned with a known efficiency-performance Pareto frontier. The outcome is a principled design methodology that moves beyond incremental modifications to ViT/CLIP, yielding architectures that are both high-performing and inherently efficient by construction.

ideas.title: A Dynamic, Heterogeneous Model Ensemble with a Specialized Routing Controller
ideas.description: This research challenges the homogeneity assumption in foundation models by proposing a system inspired by the adaptive immune system. In immunology, a diverse set of specialized cells is dynamically selected and coordinated to tackle specific threats. We adapt this solution by designing an AI architecture consisting of a large, heterogeneous library of small, specialized models and a central, intelligent routing controller. This controller, trained via reinforcement or meta-learning, analyzes each input and dynamically composes a bespoke ensemble from the specialist models, activating only the relevant 'cells' for the task. This creates a flexible, efficient, and highly adaptive system that moves beyond the one-size-fits-all paradigm of monolithic models.

ideas.title: Causal Attribution of VLM Capabilities and Biases to Data Mixture Components
ideas.description: This research applies econometric causal inference frameworks to the systematic study of Vision-Language Model (VLM) data mixtures. The goal is to construct causal models where interventions—such as varying the proportion, source, or quality of specific pretraining or instruction-tuning data components—are used to estimate their distinct causal effects on both downstream task performance and the emergence of specific social, cultural, or cognitive biases. This allows for the rigorous attribution of model behaviors to their data origins, creating a principled methodology for auditing bias and predicting capability trade-offs *before* full model training. The outcome is a data-mixing design tool that optimizes for capability while explicitly controlling for and mitigating bias based on causal evidence.

ideas.title: Explore boundaries of agentic problem solving

ideas.title: Interpretability-Guided Architectural Search for Emergent Efficiency
ideas.description: This research direction proposes using neural recording (e.g., representation similarity analysis) and ablation techniques from neuroscience to perform a causal analysis of which specific computational structures and representational geometries are necessary for emergent capabilities in large foundation models. These insights are then used as a blueprint to architecturally bias or directly design much smaller, more efficient models—such as sparse mixtures of experts or models with structured state spaces—that explicitly instantiate these critical mechanisms. The resulting smaller models are iteratively analyzed with the same interpretability tools, creating a feedback loop that tests hypotheses about the computational essence of emergence, moving beyond scale as the primary variable. The ultimate goal is to develop a theory of 'minimal sufficient circuits' for advanced capabilities, enabling efficient, transparent, and controllable AI systems.

ideas.title: Edge-Adaptive Foundation Models via Progressive Reasoning Distillation and Hardware-Cognizant Architecture
ideas.description: This research develops ultra-efficient foundation models for edge deployment by fundamentally rethinking the scaling-down process. Instead of merely reducing parameters, it uses progressive knowledge distillation to transfer the complex reasoning capabilities of a large teacher model into a compact student. The student's architecture is automatically co-designed with a hardware-aware neural architecture search, optimizing for the specific constraints (e.g., memory, latency, power) of target edge devices. The resulting models preserve robust emergent reasoning and practical utility that naive small models lose, enabling capable on-device AI without constant cloud dependency.

ideas.title: Automated Adversarial Benchmark Evolution for Testing Generalization to Novel Failures
ideas.description: This research designs a system where evaluation benchmarks are not static datasets but are generated and updated by a population of automated red-team agents. These agents use diverse strategies, including gradient-based attacks, evolutionary search, and LLM-driven prompt generation, to continuously find new model failure modes. The core model's robustness is then measured by its performance against this dynamically evolving benchmark, with a key metric being its resistance to novel attack strategies not seen in previous evaluation cycles. This creates a moving-target evaluation that directly tests a model's ability to generalize its robustness beyond a fixed set of known challenges.

ideas.title: Governance-Aware Neural Architecture Search for Auditable VLM Design
ideas.description: This research proposes a novel framework that integrates interdisciplinary governance principles directly into the Neural Architecture Search (NAS) process for Vision-Language Models. Instead of solely optimizing for task accuracy or efficiency, the NAS algorithm's search space and reward function are designed to incorporate quantifiable metrics from law, ethics, and social science, such as bias mitigation, provenance tracking for multimodal outputs, and structural explainability. This results in VLMs whose architectures are not only performant but are also inherently structured to facilitate compliance audits and impact assessments. The outcome is a dual deliverable: an optimized model and a transparent, governance-justified blueprint of its design decisions, bridging the gap between technical development and responsible deployment.

ideas.title: NAS for VLM Design via Metabolic Pathway Optimization
ideas.description: This idea applies an analogical solution from Systems Biology to the problem of using Neural Architecture Search (NAS) for Vision-Language Model (VLM) design. In metabolic engineering, algorithms like OptKnock use constraint-based modeling (e.g., Flux Balance Analysis) to efficiently search the combinatorial space of possible metabolic pathway modifications. They simulate metabolite flow to identify optimal network connections that maximize a biochemical objective. Adapted to VLM design, we propose a NAS framework that treats the visual and language processing streams as an information metabolic network. A constraint-based simulator would model the flow and transformation of multimodal information (features, tokens, attentions) between candidate modules. The NAS algorithm, guided by this simulator, would search for optimal architectures by 'knocking out' inefficient connections, 'overexpressing' critical pathways, and discovering novel fusion points, all to maximize a downstream task performance metric under defined computational budgets, thereby automating the discovery of superior VLM connectivity patterns.

ideas.title: Causal Discovery-Informed Mediation for Vision-Language Interpretability
ideas.description: This approach first employs causal discovery techniques to automatically infer the latent causal structure linking visual feature representations to generated language tokens within a multimodal model. The discovered graph then rigorously defines the mediation pathways for a subsequent causal mediation analysis, which quantifies the direct and indirect effects of specific visual concepts. This two-stage process mitigates the risk of model misspecification by grounding the causal assumptions in data-driven evidence from the model's internal activations. The result is a more robust and empirically justified interpretability framework that explains not just 'how much' a feature matters, but 'how' it causally influences the output through the model's computational graph.

ideas.title: Establish scaling laws for VLMs using principles from pharmacology dose-response modeling
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution from pharmacology dose-response modeling to establish scaling laws for Vision-Language Models (VLMs). In pharmacology, the effect of a drug is quantified by systematically varying parameters like dose and exposure time and fitting sigmoidal models to predict outcomes. Analogously, we propose treating scaling parameters (model size, data volume, compute budget) as inputs to be varied, and VLM performance on a battery of multimodal benchmarks as the output response. The adapted idea is to design a large-scale, controlled experimental matrix across these dimensions to fit empirical scaling laws, likely using a generalized form of a scaling equation. This will map the 'response surface' of VLM capabilities, identifying optimal scaling pathways, diminishing returns, and performance thresholds, thereby providing a predictive framework for efficient resource allocation in VLM development.

ideas.title: Engineered Micro-Emergence: A Curriculum and Task Suite for Inducing Phase Transitions in Small Models
ideas.description: This project proactively designs for emergence in sub-100M parameter models, rejecting the premise that it is solely a function of scale. We develop a 'Complexity-Graded Emergence Suite' (CGES) of interrelated tasks where solving simpler ones is necessary but insufficient for harder ones, creating a clear capability cliff. A curriculum training protocol then exposes models to data and tasks in a controlled sequence, while architectural interventions like dynamic sparse training are used to modulate effective capacity. The goal is to reliably produce and study sharp phase transitions in small models, turning emergence from an observed phenomenon into a reproducible engineering outcome.

ideas.title: Apply Model Checking from Hardware Design to Verify Foundation Model Behavioral Properties
ideas.description: This idea adapts the formal verification technique of model checking, widely used in digital hardware design, to the domain of foundation model safety. In hardware, engineers model a system's states and transitions and use automated tools to exhaustively verify temporal logic properties (like liveness and safety). The adapted approach involves constructing a tractable, abstract state-machine model that captures essential behavioral dynamics of a foundation model (such as interaction sequences and internal reasoning checkpoints). Critical safety and correctness properties—for instance, 'the model always cites a source before making a factual claim if instructed to do so' or 'harmful content generation is always preceded by a detectable internal refusal conflict'—are formally specified in a temporal logic. A model checker then rigorously verifies these properties against the abstract behavioral model. If a property is violated, the tool produces a trace (a sequence of model states and inputs) that serves as a concrete, adversarial test case to diagnose and mitigate the failure in the actual model, providing a rigorous, automated method for guaranteeing specific high-stakes behavioral constraints.

ideas.title: Unified Defense Against Synergistic Training-Time Poisoning and Inference-Time Extraction Attacks
ideas.description: This research addresses the critical, underexplored threat where data poisoning and extraction attacks are combined by an adversary to breach model integrity and privacy more effectively. We design a system that models a strategic attacker who poisons training data to create 'privacy backdoors,' making specific memorized data far more susceptible to later extraction. The core of the approach is a closed-loop adversarial audit framework that continuously generates and tests these combined attack strategies against the target model. The insights from this audit train a unified mitigation module that simultaneously sanitizes training data and constrains model outputs to be robust against this amplified, two-stage privacy threat.

ideas.title: Failure-Driven Dynamic Data Distillation for Robust Dataset Pruning
ideas.description: This research investigates a dynamic, closed-loop system for reducing dataset size that directly counteracts the generalization brittleness caused by curation bias. Instead of a single curation step, the method uses a small pilot model trained on an initial curated subset to actively diagnose its own knowledge gaps by evaluating performance on the full, uncurated data pool. The system then selectively mines and adds the most informative failure cases from this pool back into the training set in an iterative distillation process. This ensures the final, much smaller dataset is not just 'high-quality' in a static sense, but is explicitly optimized to cover the diverse failure modes necessary for robust model generalization.

ideas.title: Challenge necessity of massive scale for emergence
ideas.description: Explore if emergent capabilities can be achieved with radically smaller, more efficient models.
ideas.sourceTitle: On the Opportunities and Risks of Foundation Models

ideas.title: Biomimetic Pruning: Creating Ultra-Efficient Foundation Models via Neural Sparsification
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution from developmental neurobiology—where an initial overproduction of neural connections is followed by activity-dependent pruning to create an efficient mature network—to the problem of creating ultra-efficient foundation models. The analogous solution is the biological process of synaptic pruning. The adapted idea is to develop foundation models with drastically reduced parameters for edge devices by first training a large, conventional model and then implementing a biomimetic, multi-stage pruning algorithm. This algorithm would systematically identify and remove redundant parameters and connections based on their contribution to task performance and activation sparsity, effectively 'sculpting' the over-parameterized network into a sparse, efficient, and high-performing architecture that retains the original model's core capabilities while being orders of magnitude smaller and suitable for deployment on resource-constrained edge hardware.

ideas.title: Develop a Statistical Mechanics Framework for Emergent Capabilities in Foundation Models
ideas.description: This research proposes to develop a theoretical framework for emergent capabilities in foundation models by direct analogy to statistical mechanics. In physics, properties like fluidity or magnetism emerge from the collective behavior of countless particles and are rigorously described by statistical mechanics, which connects micro-level interactions to macro-level phenomena via scaling parameters and phase transitions. The analogous solution is to treat a foundation model's architecture and training dynamics as a complex system with many interacting components (parameters, neurons). The adapted idea is to create a formalism where model scale (parameters, compute, data) acts as a control parameter. The goal is to derive statistical 'partition functions' for model families, identify 'order parameters' that quantify nascent capabilities, and theoretically predict critical scaling thresholds where discontinuous jumps in capability (emergent phenomena) should occur, moving from descriptive observation to predictive theory.

ideas.title: Ecological Resilience-Inspired Benchmarks for AI System Robustness
ideas.description: This research develops a new class of evaluation benchmarks that apply principles from ecological resilience theory—such as functional redundancy, response diversity, and modularity—to assess AI system robustness. Instead of testing individual models in isolation, the benchmarks simulate environmental stressors and adversarial perturbations across populations of AI agents to measure the ecosystem's overall fragility and capacity for adaptive recovery. This allows for the systematic characterization of failure modes that emerge from model homogenization and interdependence, like correlated errors or cascading collapses. The outcome is a predictive toolkit to guide the development of more diverse, resilient, and sustainable AI ecosystems before widespread deployment.

ideas.title: Apply curriculum learning strategies
ideas.description: Structure training with a curriculum from simple to complex spatial concepts, as used in cognitive science.
ideas.sourceTitle: SpatialVLM: Endowing Vision-Language Models with Spatial Reasoning Capabilities

ideas.title: Data Distillation via Information-Theoretic Curation
ideas.description: This research investigates if extreme data curation, inspired by 'pure component' analysis in analytical chemistry, can reduce dataset size while maintaining model performance. In spectroscopy, complex datasets are reduced to a minimal set of fundamental components that explain the overall variance. We adapt this solution by proposing to algorithmically decompose a large, representative sample of training data (e.g., images, text tokens) to identify a small, curated set of 'informationally dense' archetypes or canonical examples. The core hypothesis is that training a model exclusively on this distilled set—which captures the essential statistical building blocks of the full data distribution—can achieve comparable performance to training on the full, massive dataset, thereby challenging scaling laws focused on raw data quantity.

ideas.title: Theoretical Frameworks for Emergence via Algorithmic and Data Efficiency
ideas.description: This research program aims to develop a unified theoretical framework that explains emergent capabilities not as a sole function of parameter count, but as a complex interplay between model architecture, training data composition, and learning algorithms. The core objective is to identify the minimal sufficient conditions—such as specific data curricula, algorithmic innovations, or architectural inductive biases—that can elicit emergent behaviors in models of radically smaller scale. This would enable the principled design of efficient models that match or exceed the capabilities of larger, less efficient counterparts, moving beyond empirical scaling laws to a theory of directed emergence.

ideas.title: Prioritize sample efficiency over final performance
ideas.description: Focus on how pre-training reduces downstream training steps, not just absolute capability.
ideas.sourceTitle: Open X-Embodiment: Robotic Learning Datasets and RT-X Models : Open X-Embodiment Collaboration0

ideas.title: Compositional Compliance Auditing for Heterogeneous Model Ensembles
ideas.description: This research direction develops automated systems to assess the societal impact and legal compliance of dynamically composed ensembles of small, heterogeneous AI models. Instead of auditing a single foundation model, the system continuously monitors the inputs, outputs, and interactions of the constituent models within a workflow, evaluating compliance across jurisdictions and ethical frameworks in real-time. This enables proactive mitigation, such as rerouting tasks to alternative compliant sub-models or triggering human review, based on the specific risk profile of the compositional operation. The result is a scalable governance layer that makes heterogeneous, modular AI systems inherently auditable and adaptable to evolving legal and ethical standards.

ideas.title: Adapting Portfolio Theory from Finance to Quantify and Manage AI Homogenization Risk
ideas.description: This research adapts the core problem-solving framework from Financial Economics—specifically Modern Portfolio Theory (MPT)—to address AI homogenization risks. The analogous problem in finance is managing systemic risk in an investment portfolio; the solution involves mathematical modeling of covariance between assets to construct diversified portfolios that are more resilient. We adapt this by treating foundational AI models, their training pipelines, and operational objectives as assets with quantifiable 'performances' and, crucially, 'failure correlations.' The research will develop metrics for AI model covariance (e.g., likelihood of simultaneous failure on novel inputs or under adversarial conditions) and define an 'AI Portfolio Efficient Frontier.' This will provide a rigorous, quantitative framework for policymakers, developers, and auditors to evaluate the systemic risk of the current AI ecosystem and to design interventions—such as incentives for architectural diversity or dataset variation—that move the portfolio toward an optimal balance of aggregate capability and resilience, thereby directly managing the risks of homogenization modeled through ecological resilience theory.

ideas.title: Probing for Emergent Capabilities via Stress-Testing and Anomaly Detection
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that multi-task evaluation is sufficient by drawing an analogy from materials science. There, standard benchmarks (like hardness tests) fail to predict real-world failures, so engineers use stress-testing and non-destructive evaluation (e.g., acoustic emission monitoring) to find emergent flaws. Adapted to AI, the research proposes to move beyond standard VQA/localization benchmarks. Instead, it designs a suite of visual-linguistic 'stress-tests'—inputs with extreme contradictions, impossibilities, or corrupted signals—and uses probes to monitor internal model states for anomalous 'emissions' (e.g., sudden representation shifts or inconsistencies). The goal is to detect emergent capabilities or latent flaws not captured by standard performance metrics, focusing on how the system fails or behaves oddly under non-standard pressure rather than how well it performs on known tasks.

ideas.title: Immutable Foundation Models with Dynamic, Attack-Resistant Adaptation Interfaces
ideas.description: This research direction proposes developing foundation models with a permanently immutable, verified core that is never fine-tuned after initial secure training. Adaptation is achieved exclusively through non-parametric methods—such as advanced prompting, learned input/output transformations, or external adapter modules—that act as a mandatory 'security and adaptation layer' between users and the core model. This architecture fundamentally limits data poisoning attacks to the disposable interface layers, while making extraction attacks on the original training data exponentially harder due to the fixed, unmodifiable core. The synergy creates a new paradigm where security is architecturally enforced by the adaptation method itself, making the model more robust as it is adapted and deployed.
