ideas.title: A Formal Artificial Neurophysiology Framework with Ground-Truth Validation
ideas.description: This research develops a rigorous, mathematically defined framework for applying neuroscience-inspired interpretability methods to foundation models, explicitly avoiding false biological analogies. It establishes formal definitions for operations like 'ablation' and 'recording' within the context of artificial networks, linking them to concepts from causal abstraction and mutual information. The core of the approach is validation using synthetic neural networks with architecturally embedded, known ground-truth circuits, allowing for precise benchmarking of interpretation methods. This ensures that insights about model internals are derived from sound computational principles rather than suggestive but misleading biological metaphors.

ideas.title: Immunological Auditing and Adaptive Sanitization for Foundation Models
ideas.description: This research draws an analogy from immunology to enhance defenses against data poisoning and private data extraction in foundation models. In immunology, a layered defense combining innate barriers with an adaptive, memory-based system trained by controlled exposure (vaccination) provides robust protection. The adapted solution proposes an 'immunological' auditing framework for AI training. It implements a static, 'innate' defense layer for initial data filtering, coupled with a dynamic 'adaptive' layer—a continuously updated auditor model trained on curated, safe examples of known attack vectors (analogous to vaccines). This auditor model actively monitors and sanitizes both the training data flow to prevent poisoning and the inference outputs to prevent data extraction, creating a learned, evolving defense mechanism.

ideas.title: Data Extraction Risk Assessment and Mitigation Co-Design for Foundation Models
ideas.description: This research moves beyond merely studying what data can be extracted to creating a comprehensive framework for preventing extraction. It first establishes a rigorous, standardized benchmark for quantifying data memorization and extraction risks across model architectures and training scales. Concurrently, it develops and integrates efficient training-time mitigation algorithms designed to minimize memorization of sensitive or copyright-protected data while preserving model utility. The output is a practical toolkit that enables model developers to proactively assess and bound data extraction risks, making safer model release a fundamental part of the training pipeline rather than a retrospective audit.

ideas.title: Adapting Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA) for AI Model Evaluation
ideas.description: This research adapts the Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA) methodology from Systems and Reliability Engineering to the evaluation of AI models. The analogous solution is a structured, proactive process for identifying potential failure points, assessing their severity, likelihood, and detectability, and calculating a Risk Priority Number to guide mitigation. The adapted idea creates a new evaluation benchmark that systematically catalogs AI failure modes—such as vulnerability to adversarial perturbations, performance degradation under distribution shift, or specific logical fallacies—and scores them along the three FMEA dimensions. This results in a standardized, quantitative risk profile for any model, moving beyond aggregate accuracy to characterize and prioritize its specific weaknesses and robustness gaps, thereby guiding more targeted improvements.

ideas.title: Genomic Forensics for Model Training Data: Applying Paleogenomic Contamination Detection
ideas.description: This research adapts solutions from paleogenomics, where the core problem is separating authentic ancient DNA from modern contaminants, to the AI domain. The analogous problem is determining what specific training data 'contaminants' can be extracted or inferred from a released foundation model. The adapted idea is to systematically develop a suite of 'genomic forensic' techniques for models. By treating the model's weights as a mixed genomic record, we can design probes and statistical tests that identify signatures of specific data subsets—like proprietary datasets or private information—based on their unique 'mutational' impact on the model's features, activation patterns, or output distributions. This provides a principled, methodology-driven framework for the inverse problem of data extraction, moving beyond ad-hoc attacks to a general science of model data archaeology.

ideas.title: Incentivizing Architectural Diversity via Standardized Interoperability and Composability
ideas.description: This research addresses the root cause of architectural homogenization by making diversity economically and practically viable. It designs a unified framework with open standards for model communication, knowledge transfer, and component assembly across fundamentally different architectures. The system includes benchmarks and tooling to train and deploy ensembles or pipelines that leverage the distinct strengths of diverse models seamlessly. This shifts diversity from a research curiosity into a deployable strategy that mitigates systemic risk while preserving efficiency and interoperability.

ideas.title: Architectural Basis Recovery: A Sparse Approximation Framework for Optimal Model Compression
ideas.description: This research formalizes model compression as the problem of recovering a minimal, computationally efficient student architecture and its parameters from a trained teacher model, framed as sparse approximation in a function space. The teacher's computational graph and activation patterns are analyzed as an overcomplete basis, and compression is the search for a maximally sparse, lower-dimensional sub-basis that can reconstruct the teacher's mapping within a specified error tolerance. The approach provides theoretical recovery bounds based on the coherence of the teacher's representations and yields practical, architecture-aware compression algorithms that jointly optimize model size, topology, and parameters against a information-theoretic distortion measure.

ideas.title: Formally Verified, Scalable Compliance Oracles for Foundation Models
ideas.description: This research direction develops a framework to translate complex, high-stakes legal and ethical requirements (e.g., from copyright law, non-discrimination statutes, or safety regulations) into formal, machine-verifiable specifications. It then builds scalable automated systems that can rigorously audit foundation model behaviors and outputs against these specifications, providing mathematically sound proofs of compliance or concrete counter-examples of violations. This moves beyond probabilistic auditing and ad-hoc testing to offer certifiable, explainable assurances for regulators and developers. The core challenge is bridging the gap between ambiguous human-centric norms and precise formal logic while operating at the scale of modern model deployment.

ideas.title: Adapting Epidemiological Causal Attribution for AI Bias Auditing
ideas.description: This idea adapts causal inference techniques from epidemiology to the problem of auditing bias in AI models. In epidemiology, rigorous methods are used to attribute health outcomes to specific exposures while controlling for confounders. Here, we analogously treat the AI model as an 'exposure' and its outputs (e.g., loan denials, resume rankings) as 'outcomes.' The goal is to causally attribute disparities in these outcomes to the model's processing, distinct from pre-existing biases in the input data. By employing epidemiological tools like counterfactual analysis and propensity score matching on observational datasets, we can estimate the model's causal effect on creating or amplifying bias, providing a more rigorous, attributable audit than standard correlational fairness metrics.

ideas.title: Use causal mediation analysis for interpretability

ideas.title: Adversarial Scenario Testing for Emergent Societal Risk in Foundation Models
ideas.description: This research designs a scalable, evidence-generating system to uncover complex, emergent societal risks from foundation models that static audits miss. It employs a hybrid methodology where automated tools generate a vast space of potential failure scenarios—spanning legal, ethical, and social domains—which are then stress-tested by structured human red teams in interactive simulations. The system outputs are not simple compliance scores, but detailed causal analyses of failure modes, their potential scale, and affected stakeholders, providing robust, actionable intelligence for governance. This shifts the paradigm from retrospective auditing to proactive risk discovery and resilience building.

ideas.title: Dual: study model compression as an inverse using thermodynamic cycle analysis
ideas.description: This idea adapts the framework of thermodynamic cycle analysis to model scaling. The original concept of treating compression as the inverse of scaling is formalized by positing an ideal, reversible 'scaling cycle' analogous to a Carnot cycle. In this cycle, scaling a model up (adding parameters/compute) and then perfectly compressing it down would return the model to its exact original state with no performance loss on a task. Real-world compression (pruning, quantization, distillation) is then the irreversible, lossy version of this cycle. The adapted research program is to 1) formally define metrics for 'compression irreversibility' (the information/performance loss inherent to a compression method compared to the ideal), and 2) develop compression algorithms explicitly designed to minimize this irreversibility, thereby making the scaling-compression duality more exact and predictable.

ideas.title: Apply Circuit Analysis from Electronics to Interpret Foundation Model Subnetworks
ideas.description: This idea adapts the problem-solving approach from electrical engineering's circuit analysis to the interpretability of foundation models. In electronics, complex devices are understood by probing signal nodes and performing 'cut-and-test' analysis on sub-circuits to trace function. The analogous solution is adapted by conceptualizing foundation model layers (e.g., attention heads, MLP blocks) as components in a computational circuit. The research would involve systematically ablating or inhibiting connections between these components while recording changes in internal activations and final outputs. This method aims to move beyond correlational interpretability to causally map discrete functional subnetworks responsible for specific capabilities, such as factual recall or logical reasoning, within the larger model.

ideas.title: Causal Discovery and Robust Estimation for Bias Auditing Under Model Uncertainty
ideas.description: This system addresses the fundamental problem of causal model specification in bias auditing by integrating automated causal discovery with robust estimation. It first generates an ensemble of plausible causal graphs from the data and domain knowledge, rather than relying on a single assumed model. For each candidate model, it calculates bias estimates using assumption-lean, non-parametric methods, and finally aggregates results with explicit quantification of uncertainty stemming from graph selection. This approach provides auditors with a rigorous, transparent range of possible bias magnitudes, preventing overconfident conclusions from a potentially misspecified single model.

ideas.title: Develop Enforceable Compliance Artifacts for Foundation Model Lifecycle Governance
ideas.description: This research designs and prototypes concrete, technical-legal artifacts that operationalize governance principles within the foundation model supply chain. It creates standardized schemas for model cards, system cards, and impact assessments that are both machine-readable and legally cognizable, enabling automated auditing and contractual enforcement. The work involves close collaboration between ML engineers, legal scholars, and standards bodies to ensure the artifacts are technically feasible, legally valid, and can be integrated into procurement contracts and regulatory frameworks. The outcome is a toolkit of binding governance instruments that move beyond high-level frameworks to implement actionable accountability.

ideas.title: Adapting the Institutional Review Board (IRB) model for foundation model deployment governance
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from the long-established governance of biomedical research, this idea adapts the Institutional Review Board (IRB) model to the domain of foundation AI models. In biomedical research, IRBs are interdisciplinary committees (involving law, ethics, and social science experts) that provide mandatory, pre-deployment ethical review. The adapted research idea is to develop and pilot 'Model Deployment Review Boards' (MDRBs) as a concrete governance mechanism. An MDRB would be a constituted body required to evaluate deployment plans for foundation models in sensitive sectors (e.g., healthcare, criminal justice, education) against a framework of legal, ethical, and social criteria, granting approval only if risks are adequately mitigated, thereby operationalizing interdisciplinary governance before real-world impact.
